Option D is incorrect for the same reason and also relies on Parameter Store, which lacks the required automatic rotation capability.

138.text:
Option A is correct because the dropping cache hit ratio is caused by query strings that differ only in parameter ordering and letter casing, which CloudFront treats as distinct cache keys. Deploying a Lambda@Edge function on the viewer request trigger to sort the query parameters by name and force them to lowercase normalizes every URL into a canonical form, so equivalent requests map to a single cache entry. This raises the cache hit ratio quickly without changing the origin application.

Option B is incorrect because disabling query-string-based caching could break application behavior that depends on those parameters and is a blunt workaround rather than a correct normalization. Option C is incorrect because deploying a reverse proxy to rewrite emitted URLs requires application changes and only addresses URLs the application generates, not normalization at the edge, and it is slower to implement. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront does not offer a 'casing-insensitive query string processing' setting; no such native option exists, so it cannot solve the problem.

139.text:
Option B is correct because a Service Control Policy applied at the organization root is the most operationally efficient way to enforce a guardrail across hundreds of accounts centrally. An SCP that denies s3:CreateAccessPoint unless the s3:AccessPointNetworkOrigin condition key evaluates to VPC ensures that any access point created by any product team in any account is restricted to VPC-only origin, with no per-account configuration to maintain.

Option A is incorrect because an S3 access point resource policy governs access to an existing access point, not the creation action, and it cannot be applied preemptively across all accounts. Option C is incorrect because using CloudFormation StackSets to push an IAM allow policy into every account is far more operationally heavy to deploy and maintain than a single SCP, and an allow policy does not prevent other policies from permitting internet-origin access points. Option D is incorrect because a bucket policy controls access to bucket objects and cannot govern the s3:CreateAccessPoint management action organization-wide.

140.text:
Option D is correct because DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is the purpose-built, fully managed in-memory cache for DynamoDB, removing the self-hosted Redis bottleneck and scaling to absorb the read spike during content updates. An Auto Scaling group provides resilient, scalable EC2 capacity, and a CloudFront distribution with the Auto Scaling group as origin caches and offloads content (which changes only four times a year) at the edge, dramatically reducing origin load during update surges. Updating the Route 53 record to alias the CloudFront distribution provides high availability and global performance, and pre-scaling before known update windows handles the predictable burst.

Option A is incorrect because fronting the application with only an ALB (no CloudFront edge caching) leaves the origin exposed to the full update-driven load surge. Option B is incorrect because keeping ElastiCache for Redis still requires managing a cache layer for DynamoDB when DAX integrates natively, and manual scaling is less reliable. Option C is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached is not the native DynamoDB cache and the ALB-only front end again misses CloudFront's edge offload for the static-ish content.

141.text:
Options B, C, and E are correct because uploading files directly to Amazon S3 and using S3 event notifications to enqueue messages in an Amazon SQS standard queue provides a durable, virtually unlimited buffer that absorbs thousands of uploads per minute during the daily peak. AWS Lambda functions triggered by messages in the SQS queue scale automatically and concurrently to perform image processing, paying only for actual usage during the burst and idling otherwise. Amazon SNS then sends a push notification to the mobile app when processing completes, which is exactly what SNS mobile push is designed for.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon MQ is a managed message broker for migrating existing JMS/AMQP applications and adds operational overhead versus the serverless SQS, which scales more naturally for this spiky load. Option D is incorrect because S3 Batch Operations performs bulk operations on existing S3 objects on a schedule, not event-driven per-upload image processing. Option F is incorrect because Amazon SES sends email, not mobile push notifications, so it cannot deliver the in-app notification the requirement specifies.

142.text:
Option B is correct because the queries are short (always under 15 minutes), run only during a five-hour evening window, and scan large volumes of data, which makes a persistent EMR cluster wasteful. Storing the data in Amazon S3 and querying it with Amazon Athena using the AWS Glue Data Catalog is fully serverless: there is no cluster to run or pay for when idle, and Athena charges only for data scanned per query. Athena natively reads ORC files and runs standard SQL, exactly matching the requirement at the lowest cost.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift Spectrum requires a running Redshift cluster, which adds ongoing cost and management compared to the fully serverless Athena. Option C is incorrect because storing data in EMRFS and continuing to use Presto on Amazon EMR keeps the expensive persistent cluster running, which is the exact problem being solved. Option D is incorrect because loading data into a provisioned Amazon Redshift cluster incurs continuous cluster cost and data-loading overhead, far less cost-effective for intermittent ad hoc queries.

143.text:
Option B is correct because creating an Auto Scaling group with a launch template, attaching it to the existing ALB, and attaching the existing EC2 instances to that Auto Scaling group introduces automated self-healing without deleting the current load balancer or instances. The Auto Scaling group performs health checks and automatically replaces failed instances, eliminating the manual operations step, and because the existing ALB and running instances are preserved and simply enrolled, the cutover causes essentially no downtime.

Option A is incorrect because deleting the existing ALB forces DNS or client reconfiguration and creates downtime during the switch to a brand-new load balancer. Option C is incorrect because deleting both the ALB and the EC2 instances causes a full outage while the Auto Scaling group launches fresh instances, the opposite of minimizing downtime. Option D is incorrect because an ALB does not register existing EC2 instances with an Auto Scaling group; the relationship is the reverse, so the instances would never be enrolled and the solution would not function.

144.text:
Option B is correct because the company already has a transit gateway in the shared services account, and the operationally cleanest way to let a frequently rebuilt development account connect is to share that existing transit gateway with the development account using AWS Resource Access Manager. The development team can then create (and recreate) a transit gateway attachment from their VPC whenever needed, and turning on automatic acceptance means each new attachment is accepted without manual intervention, supporting the frequent teardown-and-rebuild pattern.

Option A is incorrect because transit gateway peering is intended for connecting separate transit gateways (often across Regions or for segmentation), adding an unnecessary second transit gateway and complexity rather than reusing the existing one. Option C is incorrect because a VPC endpoint exposes a specific PrivateLink service, not general VPC-to-VPC connectivity to the shared services applications via the transit gateway. Option D is incorrect because building an EventBridge-plus-Lambda auto-accept mechanism is custom, higher-overhead engineering, and AWS Network Manager is for visualizing and managing global networks, not for sharing a transit gateway with another account.

145.text:
Option B is correct because the CloudWatch metrics showed the launch consumed only about one quarter of the expected CPU and memory, meaning the original r6g.16xlarge memory-optimized fleet was significantly over-provisioned and the workload is not memory-bound at the ratio those instances provide. Moving to m6g.4xlarge general-purpose instances right-sizes the fleet to the actual balanced CPU-to-memory consumption observed, and using a smaller instance with a sensible min/desired of 3 and max of 12 lets the Auto Scaling group track the now-variable demand cost-effectively while preserving multi-AZ capacity.

Option C is incorrect because the r6g (memory optimized) family keeps the high memory-to-CPU ratio that the metrics proved is unnecessary, so it overspends on memory the workload does not use. Option A is incorrect because c6g compute-optimized instances skew toward CPU and provide too little memory relative to the balanced consumption pattern, risking memory pressure. Option D is incorrect because r6g.8xlarge is both still memory-optimized and a large instance with a low maximum of 6, reducing scaling granularity and overspending compared to the right-sized general-purpose option.

146.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CodeArtifact is a fully managed artifact repository that supports an external connection to public:pypi, so it proxies and caches packages from PyPI on demand. By configuring the Python client (pip) to use the CodeArtifact repository and reaching it through a VPC interface endpoint (AWS PrivateLink), the SageMaker instances can pull required packages without ever routing traffic to the internet, satisfying the isolation requirement with minimal operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CodeCommit is a Git source-control service, not a Python package repository; building and synchronizing a CodeCommit repo per package is unworkable and does not serve pip installs. Options B and C both violate the core requirement by introducing a NAT gateway or NAT instance, which gives the instances a path to the internet; network ACLs and notebook firewall rules cannot reliably restrict outbound traffic to only PyPI's many CDN endpoints, and a NAT instance also adds self-managed operational overhead.

147.text:
Option C is correct because AWS DataSync provides a purpose-built, automated agent that efficiently and securely transfers files from an on-premises NFS file system. Sending the data over the existing Direct Connect connection using a public VIF to an S3 bucket keeps the heavy image transfer fast and reliable, and an S3 event notification triggering a Lambda function to copy each new object into the EFS file system creates a continuous, hands-off replication pipeline ahead of cutover.

Option A is incorrect because scripting and scheduling aws s3 sync from the on-premises server is manual and operationally heavier than a managed DataSync transfer, lacking DataSync's built-in integrity checks and scheduling. Option B is incorrect because Storage Gateway file gateway is designed for ongoing S3-backed file access, not for one-directional replication into EFS, and still requires a custom copy process. Option D is incorrect because DataSync cannot use a private VIF to reach an EFS interface endpoint for this transfer pattern, and a once-every-24-hours scheduled task would not keep the EFS copy current with images written throughout each night.

148.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams ingests the continuous sensor stream in real time with low latency, and Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL database that stores items without a fixed schema, directly satisfying both the real-time and schemaless storage requirements. A consumer reading from the stream can write the JSON readings into DynamoDB as they arrive, providing a fully managed, scalable end-to-end pipeline.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a columnar data warehouse with a fixed relational schema, and Kinesis Data Firehose buffers and batches data, so it is near-real-time rather than real-time. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a relational database that requires a fixed schema, contradicting the schemaless requirement. Option D is incorrect because, although Keyspaces (Cassandra) is flexible, Kinesis Data Firehose is a buffered delivery service that does not deliver data in true real time, and Firehose does not natively target Keyspaces, making this combination unsuitable.

149.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon API Gateway natively supports a REST API model without changing the existing API contract, and it provides built-in API keys and usage plans that map directly to the backend's existing per-key throttling and live-versus-test traffic separation. Running the business logic on AWS Lambda removes the server-capacity ceiling by scaling automatically to handle the varying peak load, and storing player session data in DynamoDB with on-demand capacity delivers single-digit-millisecond, low-latency reads that fix the session-fetch latency problem.

Option D is incorrect because AWS AppSync is a GraphQL service, which would change the REST API model the requirements say must be preserved. Options A and B are incorrect because running logic on EC2 (A) keeps a fixed-capacity bottleneck during peaks and requires managing instances; both A and D also use Aurora Serverless, which is a relational store that does not provide the same simple low-latency key-value session access as DynamoDB. Option B additionally fronts the REST API with an ALB rather than API Gateway, so it lacks the native API key and usage-plan throttling the backend depends on.

150.text:
Option A is correct because cost allocation tags must be activated centrally in the organization's management account, where the user-defined BusinessUnit tag is enabled once for the entire organization. Creating an S3 bucket and an AWS Cost and Usage Report in the management account captures consolidated, tagged cost data for all member accounts, and querying that CUR with Amazon Athena and visualizing it in Amazon QuickSight gives the finance team flexible per-business-unit cost analysis and dashboards.

Option B is incorrect because cost allocation tags cannot be activated in member accounts; they must be activated in the management account, and a CloudWatch dashboard is for operational metrics, not CUR cost data. Option C is incorrect because creating a separate CUR and S3 bucket in each member account fragments the data and adds overhead, and again CloudWatch dashboards do not visualize CUR data. Option D is incorrect because it likewise tries to activate cost allocation tags in each member account, which is not where activation occurs, even though it otherwise correctly uses Athena and QuickSight.

151.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Client VPN is the managed, client-based VPN service intended for remote individual users, which fits engineers working remotely. Creating an AD Connector directory lets Client VPN integrate with the existing self-managed Active Directory Domain Services on EC2 for authentication, and enabling MFA on the AD Connector satisfies the requirement that VPN access use multi-factor authentication, giving authenticated users private access to nonpublic services in the VPC.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Site-to-Site VPN connects entire networks (gateway to gateway), not individual remote clients, and the Amazon WorkSpaces client is for desktop streaming, not establishing this VPN. Option C is incorrect because VPN CloudHub is for connecting multiple on-premises sites to a VPC, not for remote user MFA access, and AWS Copilot is a container deployment tool unrelated to VPNs. Option D is incorrect because Amazon WorkLink was a service for mobile access to internal web content and does not provide a Client VPN with AD-backed MFA for general VPC service access.

152.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Redshift Concurrency Scaling automatically adds transient cluster capacity to absorb bursts of concurrent read queries and then removes it when demand subsides. This keeps the main cluster fully available for both read and write workloads during the audit report's complex, CPU-intensive queries, and because AWS provides a generous amount of free concurrency-scaling credits each day, it is the most cost-effective way to handle the unpredictable bursts without permanently resizing.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning a separate Amazon EMR cluster adds significant cost and operational complexity and requires moving or duplicating data. Option B is incorrect because a classic resize is slow and puts the cluster into read-only mode during the operation, violating the requirement to always service write queries. Option C is incorrect because, while elastic resize is faster than classic resize, it still briefly pauses queries, scales the entire cluster permanently rather than on demand, and is far less cost-effective and responsive than the on-demand, burst-oriented Concurrency Scaling feature.

153.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) performs block-level, agent/agentless replication of the existing VMware VMs as-is, preserving their many different operating systems and custom software packages without rebuilding anything, which is the fastest way to migrate 120 diverse VMs. Pairing it with an Amazon EFS file system and AWS DataSync to copy the 10 TB NFS data over the existing 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection lets both the servers and the file data migrate quickly and in parallel over the high-bandwidth link.

Option A is incorrect because manually exporting each VM, uploading to S3, and running VM Import/Export, plus shipping a Snowball Edge for only 10 TB, is far slower than direct replication over a 10 Gbps line. Option C is incorrect because recreating 120 VMs and reinstalling all custom software by hand is extremely time-consuming and error-prone, and FSx for Lustre is a high-performance compute file system that is unnecessary for a general NFS share. Option D is incorrect because ordering and round-tripping two Snowball Edge devices for data that fits easily over the 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection adds days of shipping delay.

154.text:
Options B, D, and E are correct because they form the AWS Service Catalog pattern for governed self-service provisioning. Packaging CloudFormation templates as Service Catalog products in portfolios within a central account and sharing those portfolios across the AWS Organizations structure (B) centralizes creation and enables distribution to multiple accounts. Granting users only ServiceCatalogEndUserAccess and using automation to import the shared portfolios locally, copy the TagOption, assign access, and apply launch constraints (D) enforces least privilege so users launch only approved products via a role they themselves lack permissions to use directly. Using the Service Catalog TagOption Library and applying TagOptions to products or portfolios (E) enforces the required tags on everything users launch.

Option A is incorrect because storing raw CloudFormation templates in an S3 bucket provides no provisioning guardrails, least-privilege launch constraints, or tag enforcement. Option C is incorrect because giving users CloudFormationFullAccess lets them provision any service the template defines, defeating least privilege, and SCPs are coarse account-level controls, not per-product approval. Option F is incorrect because the CloudFormation Resource Tags property only tags resources within a given template and does not enforce tagging across user-launched infrastructure the way the Service Catalog TagOption Library does.

155.text:
Option B is correct because AWS publishes a CloudFront-managed prefix list, com.amazonaws.global.cloudfront.origin-facing, that contains the current CloudFront origin-facing IP ranges and is maintained automatically by AWS. Referencing this managed prefix list in the ALB security group ingress restricts inbound access so that only CloudFront can reach the ALB, blocking direct ALB endpoint access with the least effort and no ongoing maintenance.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFront is a global edge service and you cannot attach a VPC security group to a CloudFront distribution or reference it as a source in the ALB security group. Option C is incorrect because an Elastic Load Balancing interface endpoint and switching the ALB to internal would break the public CloudFront-to-ALB path and is a far larger architectural change. Option D is incorrect because manually extracting CloudFront IP ranges from ip-ranges.json and maintaining the security group is operationally heavy and brittle, since those ranges change frequently and would require constant updates compared to the self-updating managed prefix list.

156.text:
Option C is correct because a cluster placement group packs instances close together within a single Availability Zone on the same high-bisection-bandwidth network segment, delivering the lowest possible network latency and highest throughput between nodes. That matches the distributed in-memory database's need for fast primary-to-worker request distribution and worker-to-worker replication, and memory-optimized instances provide the high RAM-to-CPU ratio an in-memory database requires.

Option A is incorrect because a partition placement group spreads instances across separate hardware partitions to reduce correlated failures, which increases inter-node distance and latency rather than minimizing it. Option B is incorrect for the same partition-placement reason and additionally uses compute-optimized instances, which lack the memory needed for an in-memory database. Option D is incorrect because a spread placement group places each instance on distinct hardware for maximum fault isolation, the opposite of low-latency proximity, and compute-optimized instances again do not suit a memory-intensive workload.

157.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Fargate runs the containers without the company having to administer any host environment, satisfying the requirement that they cannot continue managing Docker hosts. Mounting an Amazon EFS file system as a volume in the Fargate task definition gives all the containers shared, concurrent access to the transaction data, and EFS automatically and elastically scales its throughput to meet the unpredictable, time-sensitive transaction volume with low latency, all without code changes.

Option A is incorrect because using Amazon S3 as shared storage would require modifying the application to use object APIs instead of a shared file system, which the company cannot do, and S3 is not a low-latency POSIX shared store. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EBS volumes cannot be simultaneously shared across multiple Fargate tasks as a common file store, so the containers could not share transaction data. Option D is incorrect because installing Docker on self-managed EC2 instances forces the company to keep administering the hosting environment, directly violating the stated constraint.

158.text:
Option C is correct because a Service Control Policy with an explicit deny on IAM actions for all principals except administrator roles enforces the restriction organization-wide. Applying the SCP to the root OU propagates it to every existing and future account automatically, so the solutions architect does not need individual access to each account, meeting the requirement with the least operational overhead through a single centrally managed guardrail.

Option A is incorrect because SCPs do not grant permissions; an allow-style SCP only defines the maximum available permissions and would not deny IAM actions to non-administrators as intended, so the control would not behave as described. Option B is incorrect because building a CloudTrail-driven Lambda function to inspect and deny IAM actions is reactive, complex, and high-overhead, and Lambda cannot actually prevent an action that has already occurred. Option D is incorrect because permissions boundaries must be attached to each role in every account individually, which requires the per-account access the architect lacks and is far more operationally burdensome than a single root-level SCP.

159.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Budgets, created in the organization's management account, tracks consolidated AWS costs across all member accounts and supports a daily budget period. Configuring an alert threshold at 80% triggers a notification when spend crosses that level, and integrating with Amazon SNS delivers the email to the finance team, providing a fully managed, low-effort solution that directly meets the tracking and notification requirements.

Option B is incorrect because Trusted Advisor's organizational view focuses on cost-optimization recommendations and best-practice checks, not on alerting against a specific daily budget threshold. Option C is incorrect because AWS Control Tower guardrails enforce governance and compliance controls, not budget-percentage cost alerting, and there is no such 80% budget guardrail. Option D is incorrect because building a pipeline of per-account CUR delivery, scheduled EventBridge-triggered Athena queries, CloudWatch alarms, and SNS is far more complex and operationally heavy than simply using the purpose-built AWS Budgets feature to achieve the same alert.

160.text:
Option A is correct because outbound communication to the on-premises systems must originate from the single allow-listed Elastic IP address, and a NAT gateway is the managed service that performs source NAT for instances in private subnets. Deploying a NAT gateway per Availability Zone gives each AZ a redundant egress path, and assigning the approved Elastic IP plus automatically recreating a failed NAT gateway and reattaching that IP keeps the on-premises firewall allow-list valid while mitigating failures automatically.

Option B is incorrect because a Network Load Balancer handles inbound traffic distribution; it does not provide outbound source-IP translation for instance-initiated connections to on-premises systems. Option C is incorrect because a single NAT gateway in one subnet is a single point of failure, and the custom CloudWatch-plus-Lambda recovery adds latency and operational overhead compared with multi-AZ NAT gateways. Option D is incorrect because you cannot assign an Elastic IP to an Application Load Balancer, and an ALB does not perform outbound NAT, so it cannot present the single approved source IP to the on-premises firewall.

161.text:
Option C is correct because the Migration Evaluator agentless collector deploys directly to the VMware ESXi hypervisor and gathers inventory and utilization data for all the VMs without installing software on each guest, which is the least operational overhead for thousands of VMs with no existing CMDB. Reviewing the collected data in Migration Evaluator lets the company identify and exclude inactive servers and produce an accurate, right-sized inventory and cost projection for a cost-effective migration plan.

Option A is incorrect because Patch Manager does not deploy Migration Evaluator, and Migration Evaluator is fundamentally agentless rather than per-VM installed, so this approach is both technically wrong and high effort. Option B is incorrect because manually exporting to CSV and eyeballing disk utilization is inaccurate and labor-intensive, and AWS Server Migration Service is deprecated and not used this way. Option D is incorrect because installing the Application Migration Service agent on every one of thousands of VMs purely for discovery, then standing up Redshift and QuickSight to analyze it, is far more operational overhead than the purpose-built agentless collector.

162.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 provides eleven nines of durability and high availability for storing large important documents as a fully managed service. A bucket policy that enforces HTTPS (aws:SecureTransport) guarantees encryption in transit, while requiring server-side encryption with an AWS KMS key (SSE-KMS) provides encryption at rest using a customer-managed key that the company controls and can configure for automatic periodic rotation, satisfying every stated requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Storage Gateway in file gateway mode is a hybrid caching layer, not the primary fully managed document store, and the answer's mention of EBS volume encryption does not address durable document storage or in-transit encryption directly. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB is a key-value/document database optimized for items, not the natural choice for storing large documents, which can exceed item-size limits. Option D is incorrect because EBS volumes require managing EC2 instances and are single-AZ block storage, which is not a fully managed, highly durable and available service comparable to S3 for important documents.

163.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudTrail supports an organization trail created in the management account that automatically logs events for every account in the organization, including accounts added in the future. This single, centrally managed trail satisfies the requirement to turn on CloudTrail in all accounts with the most operational efficiency, since no per-account configuration or ongoing automation is needed.

Option A is incorrect because a daily Lambda function that creates trails in each account is a custom, fragile workaround that adds maintenance overhead and only acts on a schedule rather than instantly covering new accounts. Option C is incorrect because manually creating individual trails per account and creating new trails whenever an account is added is labor-intensive; the SCP to prevent deletion is reasonable hardening but does not address efficient enablement. Option D is incorrect because a Systems Manager Automation runbook driven by State Manager is unnecessary custom orchestration when the native organization trail feature already provides automatic, all-account coverage.

164.text:
Option D is correct because an Amazon API Gateway usage plan combined with an API key is the native, no-cost mechanism for controlling how much a specific consumer can call the API. Defining throttling limits (rate and burst) and quotas in the usage plan and associating it with the Production stage and the external team's API key caps both request rate and total usage, directly limiting cost without any changes to the Lambda functions.

Option A is incorrect because inserting SQS queues and rewriting the Lambda functions to be queue consumers reworks the functions, which the requirement forbids, and adds architecture without capping consumer usage. Option B is incorrect because provisioned concurrency increases cost by paying for pre-warmed capacity and is aimed at latency, not at limiting usage. Option C is incorrect because, although an AWS WAF rate-based rule can throttle requests, it adds the ongoing cost of a WAF web ACL and is a heavier, less precise approach than the purpose-built and free API Gateway usage plan with quotas.

165.text:
Option B is correct because an AWS PrivateLink VPC endpoint service built on the centralized application's Network Load Balancer exposes the application privately to other VPCs without any routing between their CIDR ranges. Because PrivateLink connectivity is unidirectional through interface endpoints, it works even though the business unit VPC CIDRs overlap with the shared VPC and with each other, and enabling endpoint acceptance lets the team approve only authorized business unit VPCs, satisfying the authorization requirement.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Transit Gateway routes between VPCs based on CIDR blocks and cannot handle the overlapping CIDR ranges described. Option C is incorrect because VPC peering also requires non-overlapping CIDR blocks and does not support transitive routing, so it cannot connect the overlapping business unit VPCs. Option D is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN with a virtual private gateway is intended for hybrid on-premises connectivity, is operationally heavy for inter-VPC access, and still depends on non-overlapping IP ranges for proper routing.

166.text:
Options C and D are correct because they address the two needs separately and appropriately. Mounting an Amazon EFS file system on the on-premises servers to act as the NAS share lets authors continue updating blog content with no interruption while the EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones mount the same EFS file system to serve that content, giving shared, concurrently accessible storage behind the ALB during migration. Ordering an AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized device and copying the 200 TB of archival data to it ships that large dataset to AWS far faster than transferring it over the Site-to-Site VPN.

Option A is incorrect because a weekly Lambda cron job copying from the NAS introduces stale content and delays updates rather than providing continuous shared access. Option B is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach is limited to instances in a single Availability Zone and io1/io2 volumes, so it cannot back a multi-AZ blog fleet, and weekly synchronization again delays updates. Option E is incorrect because AWS Snowcone has only terabyte-scale capacity and is wholly insufficient for transferring 200 TB of archival data.

167.text:
Option C is correct because creating an AWS IoT Core domain configuration in each Region gives each Region its own custom data endpoint, and a Route 53 failover routing policy with a health check that evaluates the domain configuration health automatically directs sensor traffic to the healthy Region for business continuity. Converting the DynamoDB table to a global table provides active-active, multi-Region replication so the time-series readings are ingested and stored durably in both Regions.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a relational database that does not naturally fit schemaless time-series readings and would require migrating away from DynamoDB unnecessarily, and the design omits Region-specific IoT domain configurations done correctly. Option B is incorrect because latency-based routing optimizes performance rather than providing failover for business continuity, and migrating to MemoryDB for Redis needlessly changes the data store. Option D is incorrect because it relies on latency-based routing instead of health-checked failover and uses DynamoDB streams for custom cross-Region replication, which is more operational effort and less robust than a native DynamoDB global table.

168.text:
Option C is correct because PGP decryption requires the recipient's PGP private key, so storing the private key in AWS Secrets Manager lets the Transfer Family managed workflow retrieve it to decrypt incoming files. Adding the decryption as a nominal (normal, always-run) step in the workflow and configuring the PGP decryption parameters there, then associating the workflow with the Transfer Family server, ensures every file the third-party supplier uploads is automatically decrypted on receipt.

Options A and D are incorrect because they store the PGP public key, which is used for encryption, not decryption, so the workflow would have no key to decrypt the supplier's files. Options B and D are also incorrect because they place the decryption logic in an exception-handling step, which runs only when the workflow fails, rather than in the nominal step that executes for every normal upload; decryption is part of standard processing, not error handling. Option C correctly pairs the private key with a nominal decryption step associated to the server.

169.text:
Options A and E are correct because a Direct Connect gateway is a global resource that lets a single Direct Connect connection (with one private VIF) reach VPCs in multiple Regions, avoiding the cost of provisioning separate Direct Connect circuits per Region. Associating the existing connection's Direct Connect gateway with the VPCs (or peering the VPCs and reaching them through a private VIF tied to the gateway) extends the on-premises reach to all three VPCs across Regions at the lowest incremental cost, while the company can use a public VIF on the same connection for AWS public service access.

Option B is incorrect because ordering additional Direct Connect connections to two more Regions adds substantial recurring port and circuit costs, the opposite of the LEAST cost requirement. Options C and D are incorrect because they introduce Site-to-Site VPN tunnels layered over VIFs, adding complexity and VPN data processing charges; a VPN over a private VIF is not a standard supported design for reaching remote-Region VPCs, and a public VIF is meant for AWS public endpoints, not for tunneling to private VPC resources, so neither minimizes cost or matches how Direct Connect gateway natively spans Regions.

170.text:
Option C is correct because a cross-Region RDS read replica continuously replicates data to the standby Region, so the data is already present and only needs promotion rather than a snapshot-copy-restore cycle. This minimizes recovery time (low RTO and RPO) because the replica stays warm; on failure, the EventBridge rule invokes a Lambda function that promotes the replica to a standalone primary and updates Route 53 to direct traffic to the pre-provisioned second ECS Fargate cluster, giving the fastest recovery.

Options A and B are incorrect because taking a snapshot, copying it cross-Region, and restoring a new DB instance is a lengthy process that greatly increases RTO; option B is even worse because it also creates the ECS cluster and service only at failure time, adding more provisioning delay. Option D is incorrect because you cannot convert an RDS MySQL snapshot into a DynamoDB global table; the data models and engines are incompatible, and this would also lose the relational structure the application depends on, making the approach technically invalid.

171.text:
Option B is correct because a Direct Connect transit VIF connected to a Direct Connect gateway, with a transit gateway in each Region attached to that gateway, delivers transitive routing between all VPCs and the on-premises data center. Direct Connect provides consistent, dedicated bandwidth and lower, predictable data transfer rates than internet egress, reducing outbound costs and improving throughput, while the transit gateway provides scalable hub-and-spoke connectivity with transitive routing that VPC peering cannot.

Option A is incorrect because VPC peering is non-transitive, so spoke VPCs cannot route to one another through the central VPC, and a VPN over the internet does not provide consistent bandwidth or reduced egress cost. Option C uses a Site-to-Site VPN as the on-premises link, which traverses the internet and offers inconsistent performance and no egress cost benefit, failing the bandwidth and consistency requirements. Option D combines VPN meshes and non-transitive VPC peering, which does not provide clean transitive routing and adds significant management overhead without lowering costs.

172.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon API Gateway with a direct service integration to an Amazon SQS queue lets each device request be durably enqueued before processing, so unpredictable traffic bursts are absorbed and no data is lost even if downstream processing lags. API Gateway scales automatically to handle spikes, SQS provides a durable buffer, and Lambda consumers scale out to drain the queue, giving a fully serverless, no-data-loss pipeline with minimal operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer cannot target an SQS queue; ALB target groups support instances, IP addresses, and Lambda, not SQS, so the design is invalid. Option C is incorrect because there is no API Gateway Auto Scaling group proxy integration, and managing an EC2 fleet adds operational burden while risking dropped requests during sudden bursts before scaling completes. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery and caching layer and cannot use a Kinesis data stream as an origin; this combination does not form a valid ingestion path and risks data loss.

173.text:
Options A, D, and F are correct because a parameterized CloudFormation template makes the stack reproducible across Regions with low administrative overhead, ensuring the second Region is built consistently. Latency-based Route 53 routing automatically sends each user to the Region with the lowest latency, improving access time while still allowing failover for disaster recovery. Creating a new DynamoDB table, enabling streams, adding the second Region to form a global table, and copying existing data is required because you cannot convert a pre-existing table that already has data into a global table without first enabling streams on a fresh table and seeding it, giving multi-Region active-active replication.

Option B is incorrect because manually documenting and rebuilding in the console is error-prone and high overhead, contradicting the minimal-administration goal. Option C is incorrect because fixed 50/50 weighted routing does not optimize access time by geography and is less suited to latency improvement than latency-based routing. Option E is incorrect as a standalone step because enabling streams on the existing populated table and adding a Region is not the supported path; the data must be migrated into a new global-table-enabled table, which is why F is required instead.

174.text:
Options B and D are correct because increasing Lambda memory also proportionally increases allocated CPU, reducing function execution time and helping clear the TooManyRequestsException throttling caused by long-running, concurrency-bound invocations. Streaming data into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams from API Gateway and processing records in batches smooths the bursty per-meter traffic, lets a single Lambda invocation write many records per batched PUT, and dramatically reduces the number of individual write requests hitting DynamoDB, relieving the ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors.

Option A is incorrect as a complete fix because simply raising write capacity treats the symptom and increases cost without addressing the inefficient per-record write pattern or the Lambda throttling; batching is the more cost-effective and durable solution. Option C is incorrect because increasing payload size sends more data per call, worsening both Lambda duration and DynamoDB write pressure. Option E is incorrect because an SQS FIFO queue that triggers a Lambda per message preserves the one-write-per-record pattern and FIFO throughput limits, so it does not resolve the DynamoDB throttling the way Kinesis batching does.

175.text:
Options A, D, and E are correct because AWS Step Functions orchestrating Lambda tasks to generate reports and write them to S3, fronted by a CloudFront distribution with an S3 origin, is a fully serverless, low-overhead way to run periodic report jobs and serve them publicly. Connecting devices to AWS IoT Core and using an IoT rule to invoke a Lambda function that parses, transforms, and stores data is the managed, scalable replacement for the self-managed MQTT collector. Migrating MongoDB to Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) preserves the document data model while offloading patching, backups, and scaling to a managed service.

Option B is incorrect because a single Lambda function polling devices does not scale to millions of devices, and a Lambda layer is for shared code dependencies, not for temporarily buffering messages. Option C is incorrect because running an EKS cluster on EC2 to prepare and serve reports adds significant cluster management overhead compared with the serverless Step Functions and CloudFront approach. Option F is incorrect because running MongoDB on EC2 keeps the operational burden of self-managing the database, contradicting the least-overhead requirement.

176.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Config managed rule encrypted-volumes detects unencrypted EBS volumes, and an automatic remediation action tied to a Systems Manager Automation runbook can recreate them as encrypted volumes, fully automating remediation. Pairing this with a KMS customer managed key whose key policy denies creating unencrypted volumes (by controlling who can call the encryption-related operations) enforces prevention, so development teams are stopped from provisioning new unencrypted volumes while existing ones are remediated.

Options B and C are incorrect because Systems Manager Fleet Manager is for managing instances and does not produce a compliance inventory of unencrypted EBS volumes or drive Config-based auto-remediation; they lack the continuous detection and automatic remediation loop that AWS Config provides. Option D is close but the EBS account-level default encryption setting only ensures new volumes are encrypted with a default key going forward and does not by itself hard-deny an explicit unencrypted volume request in the strict way the policy-based deny in option A provides for enforcing the security mandate, making A the answer that both remediates and prevents most directly.

177.text:
Option D is correct because the application is stateless and idles near 10% CPU with multi-hour spikes to 100%, so an Auto Scaling group that scales between a minimum of 4 and a maximum of 28 instances matches capacity to demand and resolves the slow responses during busy periods. Purchasing Reserved Instances for the 4 always-on baseline instances locks in savings for the steady load over the 3-year commitment, while the additional instances launch on demand only when needed, making this the most cost-effective fit.

Option A is incorrect because keeping a minimum of 20 and Reserved Instances for 20 over-provisions for the 10% baseline, wasting money since most of those instances are barely utilized. Option B is incorrect because it fixes capacity at 20 On-Demand instances with no scaling, so it neither adds capacity for peaks nor saves cost. Option C is incorrect because Spot Instances can be interrupted at any time, which is inappropriate for a customer-facing application needing reliable peak performance, and replacing the NLB with an ALB is an unnecessary change unrelated to the scaling problem.

178.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk natively supports Tomcat (Java) platforms and lets you deploy the application JAR files without source code changes, while it manages provisioning, load balancing, health monitoring, and auto scaling across multiple Availability Zones to absorb month-end traffic spikes. Storing data in Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL offloads database administration, and adding CloudFront with an Application Load Balancer improves scalability and performance, all with the least operational overhead since Beanstalk handles the undifferentiated infrastructure work.

Option A is incorrect because manually deploying Tomcat and PostgreSQL onto EC2 with EFS mounts and orchestrating scaling through Step Functions is highly manual and operationally heavy. Option B is incorrect because running EKS across multiple Regions and self-managing PostgreSQL in containers adds major complexity and is overkill for a single application. Option C is incorrect because the company lacks source code, so refactoring into Python Lambda functions and DynamoDB is impossible, and Storage Gateway is irrelevant to this application scaling need.

179.text:
Option D is correct because each sale event is a known, scheduled one-time spike, so a scheduled scaling policy proactively adds EC2 capacity at the exact known time, maximizing availability without waiting for reactive metrics. Hosting the database on an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ cluster provides high availability, and using EventBridge to invoke Lambda functions that create a larger Aurora Replica before the event, then fail over to it, scales the database vertically for the peak; another scheduled rule scales it back down afterward, controlling cost while ensuring capacity is ready.

Options A and C are incorrect because predictive scaling forecasts from historical patterns, which is poorly suited to one-time scheduled spikes that have no recurring history, so scheduled scaling is the better match. Option B (and A's data tier) use RDS for PostgreSQL with read replicas; promoting or failing over to a larger read replica is slower and less seamless than Aurora's replica failover, and Aurora better meets the maximize-availability goal, making D the strongest choice for both the compute and database scaling needs.

180.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Storage Gateway in file gateway mode presents an SMB/NFS file share to on-premises applications while storing the underlying data as objects in an Amazon S3 bucket. This lets the company copy data to the gateway endpoint so it lands natively in S3 (meeting the requirement for S3 shared storage) while the legacy application continues to access the same data through SMB until it is rewritten to use S3 APIs, bridging the migration with no application changes.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server stores data in a managed Windows file system, not in an S3 bucket, so it does not meet the requirement to use S3 as the shared storage target. Option B is incorrect because simply copying data into an S3 bucket leaves no SMB access path for the on-premises application, breaking it until the code is rewritten. Option C is incorrect because Server Migration Service migrates whole servers as EC2 instances and does not place data in S3 or provide ongoing SMB-to-S3 access.

181.text:
Options A and C are correct because a custom IAM Identity Center permission set whose policy scopes S3 access using the ${aws:PrincipalTag/userName}/* condition confines each data scientist to a prefix matching their own username, so users access only their own work in the single shared bucket. Configuring AWS CloudTrail to log S3 data events to an S3 bucket and querying those logs with Amazon Athena produces the monthly object-level access reports, because data events (not management events) capture per-object GET/PUT activity by user.

Option B is incorrect because granting the whole group blanket read/write to the bucket does not isolate users to their own prefixes, violating the least-privilege requirement. Option D is incorrect because S3 object access is recorded by CloudTrail data events, not management events, and management events would not show which documents each user accessed. Option E is incorrect because EMRFS and S3 Select are not access-logging or audit mechanisms for tracking per-user document access and do not produce the required reports.

182.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Service Catalog lets the manager package the CloudFormation template as a product and apply a launch constraint that associates the existing privileged role with the product. Testers are granted only Service Catalog permissions, yet when they launch the product, Service Catalog assumes the constrained role to create the EC2, Auto Scaling, and CloudFormation resources. This delegates provisioning without granting the testers broad CloudFormation or EC2 permissions, exactly meeting the goal of self-service with least privilege.

Option A is incorrect because allowing users to assume the manager's role grants them that role's broad permissions directly, which is the opposite of restricting access. Option C is incorrect because giving users CloudFormation and S3 permissions still exposes them to the underlying provisioning APIs and is difficult to constrain tightly to only the template's resources. Option D is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk is for deploying applications, not for launching arbitrary CloudFormation environments, and it does not provide the controlled, constrained self-service launch model that Service Catalog's launch constraints deliver.

183.text:
Option C is correct because changing the API Gateway endpoint type to private and creating an interface VPC endpoint (powered by AWS PrivateLink) makes the API reachable only from within the VPC over private connectivity. Attaching a resource policy to the API restricts invocation to that VPC endpoint, and the existing API Gateway authorizers continue to enforce authenticated access, so the APIs become VPC-only with authentication intact and with the least effort, since it reuses API Gateway's native private endpoint feature.

Option A is incorrect because fronting the Lambda functions with an internal ALB bypasses API Gateway's authentication mechanisms and requires redesigning the invocation path. Option B is incorrect because manipulating DNS and CNAME records does not make the API private or block public access; the Regional endpoint remains internet-reachable. Option D is incorrect because deploying Lambda in the VPC and proxying through a self-managed Apache EC2 instance adds significant operational overhead and a fragile custom layer, far more effort than simply switching the API to a private endpoint.

184.text:
Option C is correct because EC2 Instance Connect lets engineers connect over SSH without any pre-installed or shared key: it pushes a temporary, one-time public key to the instance at connection time via the SendSSHPublicKey API, so no two connections reuse the same key. Granting the engineers' IAM role an Allow on the ec2-instance-connect:SendSSHPublicKey action authorizes browser-based or CLI SSH from the EC2 console, and these API calls are recorded in AWS CloudTrail, satisfying both the unique-key and logging requirements.

Option A is incorrect because storing per-instance long-lived keys in Secrets Manager is operationally heavy at hundreds of instances and the actual SSH session itself is not logged in CloudTrail. Option B is incorrect because manually rotating keys through Systems Manager documents still relies on persistent keys and adds management burden. Option D is incorrect because it conflates Secrets Manager key storage with Session Manager (which does not set SSH keys), is needlessly complex, and the SSH connections themselves would not be captured the way Instance Connect's API calls are in CloudTrail.

185.text:
Option C is correct because the sensors can only upload via FTP and there is no internet connectivity, so an AWS Snowcone device running an Amazon Linux 2 EC2 instance with a self-installed FTP server gives the sensors a local FTP target to upload the 6 TB of data over the week. After the experiment, the Snowcone is shipped back to AWS, where its data is ingested into Amazon S3 object storage, meeting the requirement to centralize collection locally and move the data to S3 as soon as possible.

Option A is incorrect because DataSync over NFS does not provide the FTP endpoint the sensors require, and Snowball Edge Compute Optimized is heavier than needed for 6 TB. Option B is incorrect because a custom shell script to download from each sensor does not match the sensors' FTP-upload-only behavior, and returning data as an EBS volume is not the requested object storage. Option D is incorrect because it relies on FSx and DataSync rather than an FTP server the sensors can use, and it loads the data as an EBS volume rather than into S3.

186.text:
Option D is correct because two AWS Direct Connect connections from headquarters into one AWS Region provide redundancy (high availability) and keep traffic off the public internet, and a Direct Connect gateway lets that single connection reach VPCs in multiple Regions globally over private connectivity. This is the cost-effective design because the company avoids paying for separate Direct Connect circuits to every Region while still serving its WAN users private access to data replicated across multiple Regions.

Option A is incorrect because establishing Direct Connect connections to all Regions in use is expensive and unnecessary given Direct Connect gateway's multi-Region reach. Option B is incorrect because inter-Region VPC peering does carry traffic over the AWS private backbone but, combined with manual peering meshes, is more complex and less scalable than a Direct Connect gateway for fanning out to many Regions. Option C is incorrect because a transit VPC solution requires self-managed routing appliances and EC2 instances, adding operational overhead and cost compared with the native, managed Direct Connect gateway approach.

187.text:
Option A is correct because storing a random secret string in AWS Secrets Manager (with a Lambda function for automatic rotation), configuring CloudFront to inject that string as a custom origin request header, and creating an AWS WAF web ACL string-match rule on the ALB that only allows requests carrying the correct header value ensures the ALB serves only traffic that originated from CloudFront. Rotating the secret automatically maintains security over time, effectively preventing direct access to the ALB that bypasses CloudFront.

Option B is incorrect because matching on CloudFront's published IP ranges is brittle (the ranges change frequently, requiring constant updates) and moving the public-facing ALB into private subnets would break its role as the CloudFront origin. Option C is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store standard parameters do not provide built-in automatic rotation like Secrets Manager, and inspecting/blocking the header in the ALB itself is not how ALB request filtering works. Option D is incorrect because Shield Advanced is for DDoS protection and does not enforce origin-only access through a security group policy in the manner described.

188.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Control Tower automates the setup of a well-architected multi-account landing zone on top of AWS Organizations, applying a consistent baseline of governance through preventive and detective controls (guardrails) implemented with SCPs and AWS Config. Reviewing the included guardrails and adding Config rules where gaps exist provides the consistent management and security baseline while still allowing per-OU and per-account flexibility for differing compliance needs, and federating with the on-premises AD FS server via an IAM identity provider integrates existing identities with the least operational overhead.

Options A and C are incorrect because manually building the organization, SCPs, OU structure, centralized logging, and Config aggregators by hand is far more operationally intensive than letting Control Tower automate the landing zone. Option B is close but connecting IAM Identity Center to AD FS is an extra step that is not the simplest federation path described, and option D's combination of Control Tower automation with AD FS federation via an IAM identity provider most directly minimizes the overhead while preserving flexibility.

189.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoints with conditional forwarding rules let VPC-based applications forward DNS queries for the on-premises Active Directory namespace to the on-premises DNS servers over the existing Direct Connect connection. This is a fully managed Resolver feature that requires no servers to operate, providing hybrid DNS resolution with the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning and maintaining your own EC2 caching DNS servers across Availability Zones adds operational burden for patching, scaling, and availability that the managed Resolver eliminates. Option B is incorrect because a Route 53 private hosted zone is authoritative for its own records and pointing NS records at on-premises servers is not the supported mechanism for conditional forwarding to an external AD domain. Option D is incorrect because standing up a new domain controller in the VPC and establishing a bidirectional trust is heavy infrastructure to manage solely for DNS resolution, far exceeding the simple Resolver conditional-forwarding solution.

190.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon AppStream 2.0 streams existing Windows desktop applications to users in any HTML5 browser, so Linux users at the acquired company can run the unmodified Windows application without rehosting it natively, achieving the least development effort. Building an AppStream image with the application, running an On-Demand fleet with dynamic Fleet Auto Scaling, and authenticating users handles access centrally and scales with demand, delivering the app cross-platform via browser sessions.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning a full Amazon WorkSpaces virtual desktop per employee is heavier and costlier than streaming just the application, and it is more management overhead than needed. Option B is incorrect because an Auto Scaling group of Windows EC2 instances accessed via remote desktop requires users to have RDP clients and provides a clunky desktop experience, not a simplified cross-platform app delivery. Option D is incorrect because refactoring and containerizing the application into a web app is exactly the heavy development effort the requirement says to avoid, since the goal is to rehost with minimal change.

191.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Transfer Acceleration routes uploads through the nearest Amazon CloudFront edge location and then over the optimized AWS global network backbone to the bucket in us-east-1, significantly improving long-distance upload performance for the European users sending large, high-resolution image files. It directly addresses the geographic latency of uploads to a distant Region with a simple bucket-level configuration.

Option A is incorrect because S3 multipart upload improves reliability and throughput for very large files but does not solve the network distance latency the European users face; it is complementary at best, not the primary fix. Option B is incorrect because CloudFront accelerates content delivery (downloads) and pointing it at the EC2 application as an origin does not optimize the upload path to S3. Option D is incorrect because adding an Auto Scaling group addresses compute capacity, not upload latency, and the slow performance is a network distance problem, not an application scaling problem.

192.text:
Option C is correct because c5 instances are compute-optimized, matching the HPC processing and compute-generation Node.js workload, and using On-Demand Instances behind an Application Load Balancer ensures the game display tier stays reliably available rather than risking interruption. Migrating the on-premises Redis to a managed Amazon ElastiCache for Redis cluster provides the fast, in-memory, low-latency reads and writes ideal for a frequently changing leaderboard, optimizing application performance with a managed data store.

Options A and D are incorrect because m5 instances are general-purpose rather than compute-optimized, so they do not best serve the HPC requirement; option A also uses Spot Instances that can be interrupted, and option D's DynamoDB is slower than in-memory ElastiCache for a rapidly updating leaderboard. Option B is incorrect because, although it uses compute-optimized c5 instances, Spot Instances risk interruption for the gaming tier, and Amazon OpenSearch Service is a search and analytics engine, not the optimal low-latency in-memory store for a real-time leaderboard that Redis provides.

193.text:
Options A and D are correct because a cross-Region read replica is asynchronously replicated and can be promoted to a standalone primary in minutes, satisfying the 15-minute RTO and 2-hour RPO without rebuilding from snapshots. A Lambda function automates the promotion during failover, and a Route 53 failover routing policy with primary and secondary endpoints automatically redirects application traffic to the promoted database in the secondary Region. Together these provide a highly available, automated multi-Region recovery path.

Option B is incorrect because recreating the database from the latest snapshot is slow and would likely violate the 15-minute RTO, and snapshot age could violate the 2-hour RPO. Option C is incorrect because copying automated backups every 2 hours does not by itself create a recoverable, promotable database and adds recovery time. Option E is incorrect because a "hot standby" that is restored from the latest automatic backup only at failure time is contradictory and would not meet the RTO; RDS does not support cross-Region warm standbys in this manner outside of read replicas.

194.text:
Options D and E are correct because Route 53 only fails away from an endpoint when it knows that endpoint is unhealthy. For an alias record pointing to other resources, the "Evaluate Target Health" setting must be enabled so the parent latency record reflects the health of its targets; if it is off, Route 53 keeps returning the failed Region's records. Likewise, weighted records only become unhealthy when an associated HTTP health check fails, so if no health check was configured for the weighted records behind the stopped servers, Route 53 considers them healthy and continues routing to them.

Option A is incorrect because record weight controls traffic distribution proportions, not failover behavior; a higher weight does not prevent failover when health checks mark records unhealthy. Option B is incorrect because a single failed server in the other (still-running) Region would not block redirection to that Region. Option C is incorrect because Route 53 fully supports nesting weighted record sets under latency-based records; this is a standard, supported configuration.

195.text:
Option D is correct because running the containerized services on Amazon EKS with AWS Fargate removes the need to manage EC2 worker nodes and provides serverless container scaling behind an Application Load Balancer, directly reducing operational overhead for the product launch. Adding RDS read replicas scales read capacity for the anticipated order spike, Amazon MSK provides a fully managed Kafka cluster (replacing the self-managed Kafka on EC2), and serving static content from S3 behind CloudFront offloads traffic and lowers latency.

Option A is incorrect because it keeps EC2 instances for the application and replaces Kafka with Kinesis, forcing an unnecessary re-architecture of the streaming layer when MSK preserves Kafka compatibility. Option B has the same Kinesis re-platforming problem and still manages EC2 instances. Option C is incorrect because deploying a self-managed Kubernetes cluster on EC2 instances retains significant operational overhead for node patching, scaling, and cluster management, which contradicts the goal of reducing operational overhead compared to the managed EKS with Fargate approach.

196.text:
Option D is correct because DynamoDB global tables provide multi-Region, active-active, single-digit-millisecond data access, which suits the simple single-table read/write barcode validation pattern with the lowest latency. CloudFront places the request handling at edge locations close to users, and Lambda@Edge can run the backend validation logic at the regional edge while reading from and writing to the nearest DynamoDB replica, minimizing round trips. Route 53 maps api.example.com to the CloudFront distribution.

Option C is incorrect because CloudFront Functions are limited to lightweight viewer request/response manipulation, cannot make network calls to DynamoDB, and lack the runtime and duration needed to perform backend barcode validation and database writes. Options A and B are incorrect because hosting backends on regional ECS or EKS clusters and routing through Global Accelerator or CloudFront with cluster origins still forces requests back to a Region for processing, adding latency and far more operational overhead than the edge-plus-global-tables design, and Aurora global database is single-writer rather than active-active for writes.

197.text:
Option D is correct because the account cannot join AWS Organizations, so SCPs are unavailable, and the appropriate guardrail is an IAM identity-based policy. An IAM policy can restrict ec2:RunInstances using condition keys such as ec2:InstanceType to allow only t3.small and aws:RequestedRegion or ec2:Region to restrict to us-east-2. Attaching this policy to the developers' roles and groups enforces the limits directly within the account.

Option B is incorrect because SCPs require the account to be a member of an AWS Organization, which the policy restrictions explicitly prohibit. Option A is incorrect because it forces the account into Organizations (also prohibited) and a tagging policy does not actually prevent launching oversized instances. Option C is incorrect because purchasing Reserved Instances is a billing/capacity commitment and does nothing to restrict which instance types or Regions developers can launch; users could still launch larger on-demand instances anywhere.

198.text:
Option B is correct because migrating to Amazon RDS for Oracle preserves the relational Oracle engine with minimal conversion effort, and RDS integrates natively with AWS Secrets Manager. Secrets Manager provides built-in automatic rotation using managed rotation Lambda functions, so enabling automatic rotation with a yearly schedule satisfies the security requirement with the least operational overhead and no custom code.

Option A is incorrect because converting Oracle to DynamoDB with AWS SCT is a major re-platforming effort for a relational transactions database, and Parameter Store with a custom CloudWatch-triggered Lambda requires building and maintaining rotation logic. Option C is incorrect because running Oracle on a self-managed EC2 instance adds OS, patching, and database administration overhead, plus custom rotation code. Option D is incorrect because Neptune is a graph database that is entirely unsuitable for relational transaction data, and it again relies on a custom CloudWatch/Lambda rotation mechanism rather than managed rotation.

199.text:
Option C is correct because more than 90% of database traffic is read-only, so a cross-Region read replica of the RDS for MySQL database in the new European Region serves local reads with low latency while writes still go to the primary. Latency-based routing on the Route 53 record directs each user to the API Gateway endpoint in the Region that provides the lowest network latency, which is precisely the requirement to minimize latency for users downloading reports.

Options A and B are incorrect because using AWS DMS to perform a one-time full load (A) or full load plus CDC (B) is intended for migration rather than maintaining an ongoing read-serving replica; a managed RDS cross-Region read replica is the native, lower-overhead choice for continuous read scaling. Option D is incorrect because geolocation routing maps users by geographic location rather than measured network latency, which does not guarantee the lowest latency and can misroute users near Region boundaries; latency-based routing is the correct fit.

200.text:
Options A and D are correct because the license is bound to a network adapter MAC address and the vendor takes 12 hours to issue new files, so you must pre-provision a pool of elastic network interfaces (ENIs), request license files for those fixed MAC addresses in advance, and store them in S3. A bootstrap script then downloads the matching license and attaches the corresponding ENI to a new EC2 instance, enabling fast, highly available recovery without waiting on the vendor. For the static database IP, storing it in Systems Manager Parameter Store and injecting it via the bootstrap script keeps the configuration centralized and updatable.

Option B is incorrect because baking license files into an AMI ties one license to instances and does not let each instance pick a distinct pooled ENI/MAC. Option C is incorrect because requesting a new license at launch incurs the 12-hour vendor delay, breaking high availability. Option E is incorrect because hardcoding the IP into the AMI requires rebuilding the AMI for any change, which is far less flexible than Parameter Store.

201.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon SES is a fully managed, cost-effective email-sending service that eliminates the operational overhead of running an SMTP server. SES natively supports email templates with replacement parameters, and the SendTemplatedEmail API operation merges the customer data into the stored template and sends the message in a single call, so no S3 storage or custom merge logic is required.

Option A is incorrect because running an SMTP server on EC2 from a Marketplace AMI reintroduces patching, scaling, and availability overhead and is not the most cost-effective option. Option B is incorrect because, although it uses SES for sending, it adds unnecessary complexity by storing the template in S3 and building a Lambda merge function instead of using SES's native templating. Option C is incorrect because it still runs a self-managed EC2 SMTP server for sending while using SES only for templates, combining the overhead of EC2 with extra moving parts.

202.text:
Option A is correct because a cluster placement group packs instances close together within a single Availability Zone on low-latency, high-bandwidth network segments, which is exactly what high-throughput, low-latency inter-instance communication requires. Ensuring the instance type supports enhanced networking (ENA/SR-IOV) further maximizes packet-per-second performance and minimizes latency. Because fault tolerance is not required, concentrating instances in one placement group is acceptable.

Option B is incorrect because simply placing instances in an Auto Scaling group in one AZ with extra ENIs does not provide the optimized low-latency, high-bandwidth interconnect that a cluster placement group delivers. Option C is incorrect because partition placement groups spread instances across distinct hardware partitions to reduce correlated failures, which sacrifices the tight proximity needed for lowest latency. Option D is incorrect because spread placement groups deliberately place instances on separate underlying hardware for fault isolation, the opposite of the close-proximity, high-throughput requirement here.

203.text:
Option C is correct because converting VPC flow logs to Apache Parquet, a columnar format, lets Athena read only the columns needed for a query and skip irrelevant data, dramatically improving query performance and reducing data scanned. Parquet also compresses efficiently, reducing storage space, and using hourly partitions enables partition pruning so Athena scans only the relevant time ranges, delivering the largest performance improvement.

Option A is incorrect because recompressing gzip files to bzip2 does not change the row-based text layout and bzip2 is not splittable in a way that helps Athena; it would not meaningfully improve query performance. Option B is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds uploads and Intelligent-Tiering manages storage cost, neither of which improves Athena query performance on the existing text logs. Option D is incorrect because creating a new Athena workgroup and using engine version 2 provides only marginal gains and does nothing to change the underlying data format, partitioning, or storage footprint.

204.text:
Options A and E are correct because placing CloudFront in front of the ALB and adding a secret custom header (with a random value) that the ALB validates before forwarding ensures traffic can only reach the application through CloudFront, preventing attackers from bypassing protections by hitting the ALB directly. Deploying an AWS WAF web ACL with appropriate managed rule groups and associating it with the CloudFront distribution lets you block common web attacks and adapt rules during an ongoing attack with minimal interruption, all cost-effectively at the edge.

Option B is incorrect because deploying the full stack in two Regions with equal Route 53 weighting roughly doubles cost without actually mitigating attacks. Option C is incorrect because ECS auto scaling and a DAX cluster address performance and read latency, not attack prevention, and DAX adds cost. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache also targets database performance rather than blocking malicious traffic, so it does not meet the security and continuity requirement.

205.text:
Option B is correct because deploying the AWS DataSync agent as a Hyper-V VM uses the company's existing on-premises compute (no new EC2 cost) and leverages the 10 Gbps Direct Connect link. DataSync natively supports bandwidth throttling and scheduled transfers, satisfying the requirement to limit bandwidth and copy during non-business hours. Writing the archived single-file batches directly to S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the most cost-effective storage tier and meets the up-to-one-week retrieval requirement, since Deep Archive standard retrieval completes within 12 hours.

Option A is incorrect because it provisions an unnecessary GPU EC2 instance for the agent (added cost) and uses Glacier Instant Retrieval, which is far more expensive than Deep Archive for data that can wait a week. Option C is incorrect because landing data in S3 Standard first and transitioning after one day incurs extra storage cost and lifecycle transition charges versus writing directly to Deep Archive. Option D is incorrect because a Tape Gateway adds virtual tape management complexity that is unnecessary for simple archival file copies.

206.text:
Option B is correct because the jobs are batch simulations that are not time sensitive and can tolerate interruptions, which makes EC2 Spot Instances the most cost-effective compute choice. AWS Batch fully manages job queuing, scheduling, and dynamic provisioning of the compute environment, and the SPOT_CAPACITY_OPTIMIZED allocation strategy selects Spot capacity from the deepest, least-interrupted pools, reducing the chance of reclamation while maximizing cost savings.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda with provisioned concurrency is more expensive for sustained, large 15-20 GB processing jobs and Lambda's limits make it ill-suited to long-running batch simulations. Option C is incorrect because mixing On-Demand with Spot raises cost unnecessarily when the workload explicitly tolerates interruptions, so pure Spot is cheaper. Option D is incorrect because running the jobs on Amazon EKS adds Kubernetes cluster management overhead and the EKS control plane cost, which is less cost-effective than fully managed AWS Batch on Spot for this fault-tolerant batch use case.

207.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed, shared Windows-native file system that supports SMB and Windows NTFS ACLs, so all three instances read and write exactly the same content and access can be controlled with Windows ACLs. Spreading an Auto Scaling group across three Availability Zones with a minimum of three instances delivers high availability and fault tolerance, and a seamless domain join (via AWS Directory Service) joins the instances to the Active Directory domain with the least management overhead.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a Linux/NFS file system that does not support Windows ACLs or native SMB for these Windows instances. Option B is incorrect because FSx for Lustre is a high-performance computing file system, not a Windows file share with NTFS ACL support, and it is unsuitable for this content store.
