Option D is incorrect because it again relies on EFS, which lacks Windows ACL and SMB support, and it omits a shared Windows file system for consistent content.

208.text:
Option A is correct because using a pre-provisioning hook with an AWS Lambda function on an AWS IoT Core provisioning template lets you validate the device's unique serial number before AWS IoT activates the thing and its certificate. By including the SerialNumber parameter in the template and calling RegisterThing with the template and parameters, you control exactly when a device is provisioned. Performing this only after installation (and validating the serial number) ensures sensors cannot send data to AWS until they are installed and validated.

Option C is incorrect because registering the CA with allow-auto-registration enables just-in-time registration that would let any sensor signed by that CA register automatically on first connection, which violates the requirement that sensors must not send data until installed. Option B is incorrect because AWS IoT provisioning hooks invoke a Lambda function, not a Step Functions state machine, and StartThingRegistrationTask is for bulk registration, not per-device install-time gating. Option D is incorrect because relying on claim certificates and template parameter validation alone does not enforce serial-number validation as a gating pre-provisioning control.

209.text:
Options A, B, and E are correct because the company wants to allow services currently in use while denying a few specific ones, which is precisely a Deny list strategy (option A): keep the default FullAWSAccess SCP that allows everything and attach SCPs that explicitly deny the unwanted services. Reviewing IAM Access Advisor (option B) shows service last-accessed data so the team can determine which services are actually in use before crafting the deny rules. Defining organizational units and placing member accounts into them (option E) lets the company administer multiple accounts together as a single unit and attach SCPs at the OU level.

Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor provides cost, performance, and security checks, not per-identity service-usage history. Option D is incorrect because removing the default FullAWSAccess SCP switches to an allow-list model that would block all services not explicitly allowed, contradicting the goal of allowing in-use services while denying only a few. Option F is incorrect because there is no default "DenyAWSAccess" SCP in AWS Organizations.

210.text:
Options B and C are correct because a transit VIF connects a Direct Connect connection to a Direct Connect gateway that is associated with a transit gateway, and BGP route exchange must occur in both directions. The on-premises router must advertise its network prefixes over the transit VIF (option B) so AWS can route return traffic to on premises, and the Direct Connect gateway must advertise the VPC prefixes to the on-premises network over the transit VIF (option C) so on-premises hosts know how to reach the VPC resources. This bidirectional advertising establishes end-to-end connectivity.

Option A is incorrect because a 1 Gbps connection is sufficient for connectivity; upgrading to 10 Gbps is a bandwidth concern, not a requirement to establish the transit VIF connection. Options D and E are incorrect because MACsec (must_encrypt mode and CKN/CAK key association) provides optional Layer 2 encryption on supported dedicated connections and is not required to route VPC traffic over the transit VIF.

211.text:
Option B is correct because a private API Gateway is reached through an interface VPC endpoint (powered by AWS PrivateLink). The endpoint policy must allow the execute-api:Invoke action so callers can invoke the API, and enabling private DNS naming lets the standard API endpoint DNS name resolve to the VPC endpoint's private addresses from within the VPC. Pairing this with an API resource policy that allows access from the VPC endpoint ensures the EC2 instance can successfully reach the private API.

Option A is incorrect because apigateway:* governs management/control-plane actions rather than invocation (execute-api:Invoke), and disabling private DNS would prevent the standard API DNS name from resolving privately. Options C and D are incorrect because Network Load Balancer or Application Load Balancer with a VPC link is used for API Gateway private integrations to backend targets (the API calling into a VPC), not for granting a VPC client access to a private API; an ALB is not even supported as a VPC link target for REST API private integration.

212.text:
Option C is correct because the data set is 60 TB and the internet connection is too slow (an estimated month), so a physical transfer device is the fastest path. AWS Snowball Edge ships a high-capacity device to the data center, you load the 60 TB locally via the S3 interface, and AWS imports it into an S3 bucket. AWS DMS then migrates the data from S3 into Aurora MySQL, completing the database migration far faster than transferring over the network.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning a 1 Gbps Direct Connect connection has long lead times and 60 TB over 1 Gbps still takes many days, making it slower than Snowball. Option B is incorrect because DataSync still depends on the limited internet bandwidth, and AWS Application Migration Service is for server/lift-and-shift migration, not loading a database from S3 into Aurora. Option D is incorrect because the standard Snowball device is being retired/has lower throughput than Snowball Edge, and Application Migration Service is the wrong tool to load relational data from S3 into Aurora MySQL.

213.text:
Option B is correct because the company needs an automated TCO report without adding VMs or installing software on the existing VMs, and SNMP is already enabled. The Migration Evaluator (formerly TSO Logic) agentless collector can be installed on a single Windows EC2 instance, collect utilization data from the on-premises VMs over SNMP, and generate the TCO/business case report. Migration Evaluator is the AWS service that produces TCO reports for migration planning.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service performs lift-and-shift replication and Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations focuses on modernization strategy, not generating a TCO cost report. Option C is incorrect because Migration Hub itself does not generate the TCO report; the report is produced by Migration Evaluator's own console, so configuring Migration Hub to generate it is wrong. Option D is incorrect because there is no "AWS Migration Readiness Assessment tool" deployed inside the VPC that drives Migration Evaluator in this way; the readiness assessment is a partner-led engagement, not the automated discovery-and-TCO mechanism described.

214.text:
Options A and C are correct because URLs must continue to be published from the existing Region while processing happens in multiple Regions. Deploying the SQS queue together with the Lambda function in each other Region (option A) creates the regional processing capacity, and subscribing each Region's SQS queue to the existing SNS topic (option C) fans the published URLs out to every Region. SNS supports cross-Region subscriptions of SQS queues, so each regional queue receives a copy of every URL, the regional Lambda processes it, and results can still be written back to the S3 bucket in the original Region.

Option B is incorrect because it has the relationship backwards: you subscribe queues to the topic, not the topic to a queue. Option D is incorrect because SQS does not publish to SNS topics; the flow is SNS-to-SQS, and adding SNS topics in each Region is unnecessary since the existing topic fans out. Option E is incorrect because deploying additional SNS topics in other Regions conflicts with the requirement that URLs be published only from the existing Region.

215.text:
Option D is correct because the .NET Core components require complex orchestration with full control over networking and host configuration, which Amazon ECS with the EC2 launch type provides since you manage the underlying container instances and networking. The strongly relational data model is best served by a relational engine, and Aurora MySQL Serverless v2 is a managed relational database. Amazon SQS is the natural managed replacement for MSMQ's asynchronous queue-based messaging between components.

Option A is incorrect because AWS App Runner is a fully managed service that does not give full control over networking and host configuration, and EventBridge is an event bus rather than a direct MSMQ-style queue replacement. Option B is incorrect because Fargate abstracts away host configuration control, and DynamoDB is a NoSQL store unsuitable for a strongly relational model. Option C is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk limits host-level control, Aurora PostgreSQL is a heavier conversion than needed, and Amazon MSK (Kafka) is a streaming platform that is overkill for simple asynchronous messaging compared to SQS.

216.text:
Option B is correct because a Compute Savings Plan offers the most flexible discount, applying automatically across EC2 (any instance family, size, and Region), AWS Fargate, and AWS Lambda usage. Since the workloads span all three compute types and have unpredictable, fluctuating demand, the flexibility of a Compute Savings Plan maximizes coverage and savings. Purchasing it from the management account using the organization-level recommendation pools usage across all member accounts, so commitments are optimized against aggregate consumption and discounts are shared organization-wide.

Option A is incorrect because Reserved Instances cover only EC2 (not Fargate or Lambda) and lock in specific instance attributes, leaving the unpredictable usage poorly matched and unused commitments wasted. Option C is incorrect for the same EC2-only and rigidity reasons, and buying per member account prevents pooling savings across the organization. Option D is incorrect because an EC2 Instance Savings Plan is limited to a specific instance family in a Region and covers only EC2, so it cannot cover Fargate and Lambda or accommodate the variable demand as well as a Compute Savings Plan.

217.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon EFS provides a fully managed, NFS-compatible file system that can be mounted concurrently by many EC2 instances, satisfying the CMS requirement for shared persistent NFS storage. AWS Elastic Beanstalk with an Application Load Balancer and an Auto Scaling group lets the fleet scale from 2 to 30 instances automatically with no application changes, and .ebextensions can mount the EFS file system at deploy time. Keeping a separate Amazon Aurora MySQL database decouples the data tier from the Beanstalk lifecycle and provides durable, highly available storage that prevents data loss.

Option B is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach supports only io1/io2 volumes attached to a limited number of instances in the same Availability Zone and uses a block device, not NFS; it does not scale to 30 instances across AZs, and placing RDS inside the Beanstalk environment couples its lifecycle to the app. Option C is wrong because a scale-in lifecycle hook fires when instances terminate, which is the wrong moment to mount storage. Option D is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach is not NFS-compatible, and ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory cache that cannot replace a MySQL relational database.

218.text:
Option B is correct because AWS IoT Core is a fully managed, serverless service purpose-built to ingest MQTT traffic from millions of devices, eliminating the single EC2 broker that became a bottleneck and caused data loss. It scales automatically to handle unpredictable spikes, supports a configurable custom domain so the existing iot.example.com endpoint can be preserved by pointing the Route 53 record at the Data-ATS endpoint, and IoT rules can route messages directly to DynamoDB with no custom broker code. This removes the scaling ceiling and improves reliability with minimal operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because placing the custom broker behind an ALB and Auto Scaling group still relies on the company's self-managed broker software, which is the source of the reliability problem, and ALB is HTTP/HTTPS-oriented rather than ideal for raw MQTT at this scale. Option C is wrong because Global Accelerator and an NLB only improve network routing; the single broker instance remains the bottleneck and data can still be lost. Option D is incorrect because AWS IoT Greengrass is designed for edge/local processing on devices, not as a cloud-side ingestion endpoint for millions of sensors.

219.text:
Option B is correct because GitHub Actions natively supports OpenID Connect (OIDC), and creating an IAM OIDC identity provider lets the pipeline request short-lived credentials directly from AWS STS using sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity. The new role's trust policy can scope access to the specific GitHub repository and branch through condition keys on the OIDC token, and attaching the existing policy preserves the current permissions. This eliminates long-lived secret keys entirely with the least operational overhead because no certificates, identity stores, or extra services must be managed.

Option A is incorrect because SAML 2.0 federation is intended for enterprise identity providers and browser-based sign-in flows, not headless CI/CD pipelines, and GitHub Actions does not provide a SAML assertion suited to this use. Option C is wrong because Amazon Cognito identity pools are designed for end-user/mobile and web app authentication, adding an unnecessary component for machine-to-machine CI/CD. Option D is incorrect because IAM Roles Anywhere requires standing up and managing a private certificate authority and distributing client certificates, which is substantially more operational overhead than OIDC for a GitHub-hosted pipeline.

220.text:
Option D is correct because the root problem is connection exhaustion from many concurrent Lambda invocations opening database connections. Amazon RDS Proxy sits between Lambda and the database, pooling and reusing connections so the surge of Lambda executions during market events no longer overwhelms the database. Migrating to Amazon Aurora and adding an Aurora Replica improves read scalability and provides fast failover for availability, and RDS Proxy also reduces failover times. Together these directly address both the scalable performance and availability requirements.

Option A is incorrect because reacting to a CloudWatch alarm to add a read replica is slow and does not solve the connection-storm problem that is causing the slow responses. Option B is wrong because while Aurora and a read replica help reads, implementing a connection pool inside Lambda is unreliable: Lambda's stateless, ephemeral execution environments do not maintain durable pools across invocations the way RDS Proxy does. Option C is incorrect because Route 53 weighted records distribute DNS responses but do not manage database connections or guarantee correct read/write routing, leaving the connection-overload issue unresolved.

221.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) continuously replicates block-level data from source servers, including Windows Server VMs on vSphere, into a low-cost staging area in AWS, supporting an RPO measured in seconds to minutes that easily meets the 5-minute target. Because it replicates whole servers at the block level, the proprietary application and its data format are captured exactly without any application awareness, and DRS can launch recovery EC2 instances on demand during a disaster. It also supports failback, letting the company return to on-premises hosting afterward, all with minimal operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync replicates files on a schedule and cannot reliably achieve a 5-minute RPO for a live server, nor does it orchestrate failover. Option C is wrong because a Storage Gateway file gateway plus AWS Backup restores are file/backup-based and too slow to meet a 5-minute RPO, and they require building EC2 instances separately. Option D is incorrect because replicating only data to FSx for Windows File Server does not capture the full server, and the manual CloudFormation provisioning adds overhead without meeting the tight RPO.

222.text:
Option B is correct because it preserves the existing architecture almost exactly while making it highly available with the fewest changes: the three Linux VMs become three Amazon EC2 instances spread across three Availability Zones, Amazon EFS provides shared, NFS-mountable file storage for the critical data accessible from all instances, and an Application Load Balancer supplies the HTTP request-based (Layer 7) routing the on-premises load balancer already performed. This is a straightforward lift-and-shift that meets the high-availability requirement quickly.

Option A is incorrect because moving to ECS Fargate containers requires re-platforming the application, and Amazon S3 is object storage, not a mountable shared file system for an application expecting file access; an NLB also provides Layer 4 routing rather than the required HTTP request-based routing. Option C is wrong for the same re-platforming reason (EKS) and because FSx for Lustre targets high-performance computing workloads, not general web application file storage, and the NLB lacks HTTP-based routing. Option D is incorrect because spreading instances across three Regions adds needless complexity, EBS volumes cannot be shared across instances, and EBS does not natively offer Cross-Region Replication.

223.text:
Option A is correct because AWS DataSync is purpose-built to automatically transfer large existing datasets and continuously move newly created files from on-premises storage to Amazon S3 without custom development. After the DataSync agent is deployed on premises, configuring a task to the destination S3 bucket in ap-northeast-1 handles the 60 TB of existing images and can be scheduled to pick up the daily new images. DataSync encrypts data in transit by default using TLS, satisfying the encryption-in-transit requirement, and it manages transfer, retries, and integrity validation automatically.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose is a streaming service for record/event data, not a tool for transferring large software image files, and it would require custom integration. Option C is wrong because an AWS Snowball device is for one-time offline bulk transfer; it does not provide the ongoing automatic transfer of new daily images. Option D is incorrect because building a Site-to-Site VPN and scripting multipart uploads with the S3 API constitutes the custom development the requirements explicitly prohibit.

224.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Security Hub provides a centralized, aggregated view of security findings and posture across all accounts. Enabling Security Hub for the organization in AWS Organizations and designating a delegated administrator account lets the security team automatically onboard accounts and consolidate findings from detective controls and integrated services (GuardDuty, Inspector, Config rules) into a single dashboard. This aligns directly with the need for a centralized security state view across the Control Tower-governed multi-account environment with minimal ongoing effort.

Option A is incorrect because deploying an AWS Config conformance pack via StackSets enforces and evaluates configuration rules but does not provide the consolidated, organization-wide security findings dashboard the team needs. Option B is wrong because Amazon Detective is used to investigate and analyze the root cause of specific security findings using log-based behavioral graphs, not to provide an aggregated posture view across all accounts. Option C is incorrect for the same reason and additionally because deploying Detective through a CloudFormation stack set is a more manual, error-prone approach than the native organization integration, and Detective still is not the centralized posture tool described.

225.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS CLI authenticates with the long-lived IAM access keys, so an MFA condition on those keys alone cannot be satisfied during a normal CLI call. The correct pattern is to attach a group policy that denies S3 actions unless aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is true, then have each engineer call AWS STS (GetSessionToken) with their MFA code to obtain temporary credentials that carry the MFA context. Storing those temporary credentials in a named CLI profile means subsequent S3 commands present the MFA-authenticated session, satisfying the deny-unless-MFA policy.

Option A is incorrect because S3 bucket policies cannot interactively prompt a user for an MFA code, and using raw access keys directly never establishes the MFA-present condition. Option B is wrong because IAM groups are not principals you assume and do not have trust policies; trust policies belong to IAM roles. Option C is incorrect because, although the deny-unless-MFA policy is right, using the plain long-lived access keys directly with the CLI does not provide the MFA context, so every S3 action would be denied; temporary STS credentials obtained with MFA are required.

226.text:
Option B is correct because storing the up-to-4-GB video files as objects in Amazon S3 provides virtually unlimited, rapidly scaling, highly durable storage decoupled from the database, so uploads and downloads do not affect application performance. Migrating the user profile table to Amazon DynamoDB using AWS DMS with the Schema Conversion Tool gives a fully managed, horizontally scaling key-value store for the profile metadata, and storing only the S3 object key in each DynamoDB item links profiles to their videos efficiently. This separation of large binary content from metadata is the standard scalable design.

Option A is incorrect because storing 4 GB videos as base64-encoded strings in a database column bloats the database, hurts performance, and exceeds practical row/column limits. Option C is wrong for storing the video reference correctly but unnecessarily migrating a simple single-table profile store to Amazon Keyspaces (Cassandra), which is heavier than needed; the distinguishing flaw is the over-complex datastore choice versus B. Option D is incorrect because, like A, embedding base64 video data directly in DynamoDB items violates the 400 KB item size limit and would not store multi-gigabyte files at all.

227.text:
Options B and E are correct because the workload is sporadic and short-lived—URLs arrive infrequently and each crawl completes in under 10 seconds—which is the ideal profile for AWS Lambda. Converting the crawler to a Lambda function that pulls (or is triggered by) the SQS queue means you pay only for actual execution time and eliminate the idle EC2 instances entirely, while scaling automatically with queue depth. Storing the resulting .csv files in Amazon S3 removes the always-on, fully provisioned EFS volume in favor of pay-per-use, durable object storage that all consumers can access, further cutting cost.

Option A is incorrect because switching to large m5.8xlarge instances dramatically increases cost and still leaves instances running idle when the queue is empty. Option C is wrong because Amazon Neptune is a graph database, unsuited to storing flat .csv crawl output and far more expensive than S3. Option D is incorrect because Aurora Serverless MySQL is a relational database that is unnecessary for storing file results and costs more than S3 object storage for this use case.

228.text:
Option A is correct because configuring scan on push enables Amazon ECR to automatically scan each newly uploaded image for common vulnerabilities and exposures as soon as it arrives. ECR emits an EventBridge event when the scan completes, which can invoke an AWS Step Functions state machine; Step Functions is well suited to orchestrate the conditional, multi-step remediation of evaluating the findings, deleting the image tag when Critical or High severity vulnerabilities are present, and publishing a notification to the development team via Amazon SNS. This is fully automated and event-driven, matching every requirement.

Option B is incorrect because ECR scan results are delivered through EventBridge, not natively pushed to an SQS queue, so the described trigger mechanism does not work as written. Option C is wrong because scheduling hourly manual scans introduces unnecessary delay and cost compared to automatic scan on push, failing the immediate-inspection intent. Option D is incorrect because periodic scanning re-scans existing images on a schedule rather than inspecting each new version on push, and like option B it relies on an SQS delivery of scan results that ECR does not provide.

229.text:
Options B and E are correct because the awsvpc network mode gives each ECS task its own elastic network interface and private IP, allowing security groups to be applied directly at the task level for fine-grained, least-privilege network control rather than at the shared host. Using IAM roles for tasks grants each task only the specific AWS permissions it needs through temporary, automatically rotated credentials, embodying least privilege at the identity layer. Together they isolate both network access and IAM permissions per task.

Option A is incorrect because bridge network mode shares the host's networking and does not let you attach security groups to individual tasks, weakening network isolation. Option C is wrong because applying security groups to the EC2 instances and using instance IAM roles grants permissions and network access at the host level shared by all tasks, violating least privilege. Option D is incorrect because passing static IAM credentials into the container at launch is a security anti-pattern—long-lived credentials are hard to rotate and easily leaked—whereas IAM roles for tasks deliver short-lived, automatically managed credentials.

230.text:
Option B is correct because an AWS Global Accelerator standard accelerator provides a small set of static anycast IP addresses that customers can add to their allow lists, satisfying the static-IP requirement. By adding a standard endpoint for the NLB in each Region, Global Accelerator automatically routes each customer over the AWS global network to the optimal, geographically closest healthy Region, and the static IPs remain unchanged as Regions are added or removed. This directly meets both the static-IP and proximity-based routing requirements.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery/caching service for HTTP(S) content and does not provide stable static IP addresses for customers to allow-list; its edge IP ranges change and are unsuitable for an allow list. Option C is wrong for the same CloudFront IP-address reason. Option D is incorrect because a Global Accelerator custom routing accelerator is designed to deterministically map specific IP:port combinations to particular EC2 instances (such as gaming or VoIP sessions), not to perform geography-based routing across regional NLBs for a general SaaS application.

231.text:
Option B is correct because an Amazon Aurora global database performs storage-level (physical) replication across Regions with typical replication lag under one second, satisfying the sub-1-minute RPO, and it supports managed cross-Region failover that promotes the us-west-2 secondary to primary in well under 5 minutes, meeting the RTO. Combined with the Route 53 health checks and DNS failover already configured to us-west-2, this provides the resilient, low-RPO/low-RTO cross-Region recovery the architecture needs.

Option A is incorrect because, although an RDS for MySQL cross-Region read replica replicates data, promoting it during a disaster is a slower, more manual process that struggles to guarantee a sub-5-minute RTO and asynchronous replication lag can exceed the 1-minute RPO. Option C is wrong because a Multi-AZ RDS deployment provides only in-Region high availability and offers no cross-Region recovery at all. Option D is incorrect because a self-managed MySQL standby on EC2 in us-west-2 requires manually building and maintaining replication and failover, which is operationally heavy and unlikely to reliably meet the strict RTO and RPO targets.

232.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams ingests the high-volume sensor telemetry in real time with low latency and per-record processing. An AWS Lambda function consuming the stream can evaluate each record immediately and, when a humidity or light value falls outside the acceptable range, publish to Amazon SNS to notify the factory operations team instantly. SNS supports immediate push notifications (SMS, email, application endpoints), making this a fully serverless, real-time analyze-and-alert pipeline that matches every requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Kinesis Data Firehose is a buffering delivery service oriented toward loading data into stores like S3 or Redshift, introducing buffering latency that conflicts with the need for immediate per-record analysis and alerting. Option B is wrong because, while Amazon MSK can stream data, it adds operational overhead, and Amazon SES is an email service intended for bulk/transactional email rather than immediate operational notifications. Option D is incorrect because pairing Kinesis Data Analytics with a self-scaled ECS consumer is more complex than needed, and SES email is less suited to immediate operational alerting than SNS.

233.text:
Option C is correct because it delivers a fully serverless, event-driven architecture with near-real-time analytics. Running the microservices on Amazon EKS with AWS Fargate removes server management, migrating the retail database to Amazon Aurora Serverless MySQL provides an auto-scaling managed relational store, and Amazon Redshift Serverless gives an on-demand analytics warehouse that replaces the weekly ETL. Using Amazon EventBridge as the event bus routes incoming data from different sources to the appropriate microservices and to the analytics database continuously, enabling near-real-time updates rather than weekly batch loads.

Option A is incorrect because keeping MySQL on EC2 is not serverless, Amazon Neptune is a graph database unsuited to OLAP analytics, and SQS alone is a point-to-point queue rather than a content-based event router. Option B is wrong because Auto Scaling groups of EC2 instances are not serverless and SNS is a pub/sub notification service that cannot route data to specific consumers by event content the way EventBridge does. Option D is incorrect because Amazon AppStream 2.0 is an application-streaming service for end-user desktops, not a microservices platform, and AWS IoT Core is intended for device telemetry rather than general application event routing.

234.text:
Options D and E are correct because they address both global read performance and the read-heavy, mostly static workload. An Aurora global database performs physical cross-Region replication, placing low-latency read replicas near the global audience while keeping a single primary, and using Amazon S3 with cross-Region replication serves static content close to users; deploying the web and application tiers in multiple Regions reduces request latency worldwide. Adding Amazon Route 53 latency-based routing directs each user to the lowest-latency Region, while Amazon CloudFront caches static and cacheable content at edge locations to dramatically cut response times, and Auto Scaling groups absorb the launch traffic spike.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 cannot replace dynamic web/application servers that render the site, and Aurora logical replication is not the appropriate cross-Region mechanism here. Option B is wrong because AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated link to a single on-premises location and does nothing to accelerate a global internet audience. Option C is incorrect because downgrading from Aurora to RDS for MySQL and merely placing tiers in private subnets does not improve global latency or read scalability at all.

235.text:
Option B is correct because the cost increase comes from frequent AWS KMS API calls generated by accessing SSE-KMS objects. Switching to server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys (SSE-S3) keeps the objects encrypted at rest but removes the per-request KMS charges entirely, since S3 manages the keys natively. Creating a new bucket configured for SSE-S3 and using S3 Batch Operations to copy the existing millions of objects re-encrypts them at scale with minimal manual effort, optimizing cost with the least operational overhead while preserving encryption.

Option A is incorrect because SSE-C requires the application to supply and manage the encryption key on every request, dramatically increasing operational overhead and changing the application. Option C is wrong because AWS CloudHSM adds significant cost and management complexity and still incurs per-operation key usage, the opposite of cost optimization. Option D is incorrect because S3 Intelligent-Tiering optimizes storage-class cost based on access patterns but does not reduce KMS request charges; the objects are accessed frequently, so they would not move to cheaper tiers, and it leaves SSE-KMS in place.

236.text:
Options A, C, and E are correct because they form a no-downtime, minimal-cutover migration across accounts. Creating the RDS for PostgreSQL target and using the AWS Schema Conversion Tool to convert and migrate the heterogeneous Oracle-to-PostgreSQL schema prepares the target structure (step A). Configuring VPC peering between the two accounts' VPCs and adjusting security groups to allow the database port provides the private network connectivity AWS DMS needs to reach the source in its private subnet (step C). Running AWS DMS with full load plus change data capture (CDC) copies all existing data and continuously replicates new changes during the migration, so when replication is caught up the Route 53 CNAME is repointed to the target with no downtime (step E).

Option B is incorrect because SCT converts schema and code objects but does not load and continuously replicate the table data; that requires DMS. Option D is wrong because making the source DB publicly accessible is an unnecessary security risk when VPC peering provides secure private connectivity. Option F is incorrect because a CDC-only DMS task assumes the existing data is already loaded; without the full load it would not replicate all existing data, so the target would not be identical.

237.text:
Option A is correct because making the application multi-AZ requires redundancy at every tier. Deploying a NAT gateway in each Availability Zone with route tables that send each AZ's traffic to its local NAT gateway removes the single NAT gateway as an AZ-level single point of failure for outbound traffic. Converting the RDS for MySQL instance to Multi-AZ provides automatic failover to a standby in another AZ. Configuring the Auto Scaling group to launch across Availability Zones and setting minimum and maximum capacity to 3 ensures running instances are spread across the three AZs so the application survives an AZ failure.

Option B is incorrect because a virtual private gateway connects a VPC to on-premises networks via VPN and is not a replacement for a NAT gateway's internet egress. Option C is wrong because a single NAT instance reintroduces an AZ-level single point of failure, migrating MySQL to PostgreSQL is an unnecessary engine change, and manually launching instances is not resilient. Option D is incorrect because keeping the Auto Scaling group's minimum and maximum at 1 confines the application to a single instance in one AZ, so enabling backups does not provide multi-AZ operation.

238.text:
Option D is correct because S3 Replication Time Control (S3 RTC) is the feature designed to provide a predictable replication SLA, replicating the majority of objects within 15 minutes and backing it with replication metrics. Creating a dedicated replication rule on the source bucket that filters on the most-accurate radar station's key prefix isolates that station's objects, and enabling S3 RTC on that rule lets you monitor the maximum replication time through the published CloudWatch metrics. An Amazon EventBridge rule can then alert when replication time exceeds the 30-minute threshold, precisely meeting the monitoring requirement.

Option A is incorrect because the company already uses S3 replication rules; adding AWS DataSync is redundant, and monitoring a TRANSFERRING status does not measure per-object replication completion time. Option B is wrong because creating a second destination bucket with its own rule does not by itself provide a replication-time SLA or the precise per-object completion metric that S3 RTC gives. Option C is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds uploads to the source bucket and the TotalRequestLatency metric measures request latency, neither of which monitors cross-bucket replication completion time.

239.text:
Option B is correct because it reduces the risk of template changes causing downtime through layered safeguards. Implementing automated testing with AWS CodeBuild in a dedicated test environment catches defects before production. Using CloudFormation change sets lets the team preview exactly which resources will be added, modified, or replaced so unintended disruptive changes are caught before they execute. Adding AWS CodeDeploy with a blue/green deployment pattern stands up the new version alongside the old and shifts traffic only after validation, providing fast, safe rollback if problems appear—directly minimizing downtime.

Option A is incorrect because relying on deployment scripts to detect errors and on manual test plans is reactive and does not preview infrastructure changes or provide automated safe rollback. Option C is wrong because IDE plugins and CLI template validation only check syntax and basic correctness, not runtime resource impact, and it still depends on manual testing. Option D is incorrect because, although it introduces blue/green deployment, having operators manually log in to instances to run a test plan is error-prone, not scalable, and omits automated testing and change-set evaluation.

240.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Network Firewall is a managed, stateful firewall that supports centrally managed rule-based filtering of outbound internet traffic. Creating a dedicated egress VPC, attaching it to the existing transit gateway, and routing all accounts' outbound traffic through it centralizes inspection for the entire organization. Deploying a Network Firewall endpoint in each Availability Zone provides high availability, and Network Firewall scales automatically to handle the stated peak of up to 25 Gbps per Availability Zone while you manage one centralized rule set rather than per-account configurations.

Option A is incorrect because running an open-source proxy on an Auto Scaling group of EC2 instances is self-managed, requiring patching, scaling, and high-availability engineering, which is far more operational overhead than the managed Network Firewall. Option C is wrong because deploying a separate Network Firewall in each of the 100+ accounts defeats the requirement for centralized management and multiplies cost and administrative effort. Option D is incorrect for the same self-managed proxy reasons as A and because placing it in every account again removes the central management the company requires.

241.text:
Option B is correct because it is the most cost-effective backup-and-restore strategy that comfortably meets a 24-hour RTO and 8-hour RPO. Scheduling RDS snapshots every 8 hours and copying them cross-region satisfies the 8-hour RPO, and storing the Docker image in ECR in both regions plus using CloudFormation to deploy the ALB, ECS, and RDS stack only at failure time means you pay for nothing in the secondary region during normal operation. Standing up infrastructure and restoring from a snapshot fits easily within a 24-hour RTO.

Option A is incorrect and more expensive because it runs a full duplicate (warm standby) set of ALB, EC2, ECS, and RDS resources continuously, and RDS does not offer the described automatic multi-region replication for MySQL in this manner. Option C is incorrect because hourly MySQL backups to S3 with manual import steps add unnecessary operational complexity and cost beyond what the relaxed RPO requires. Option D is incorrect because a pilot light with a continuously running cross-region read replica and always-on ALB costs more than the relaxed RTO/RPO justify; the requirements are loose enough that a cheaper backup-and-restore approach suffices.

242.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) is the purpose-built, managed service for migrating databases to AWS, and Amazon DocumentDB is a supported target endpoint while a self-managed MongoDB replica set is a supported source. Using change data capture (CDC) lets DMS perform a full load and then continuously replicate ongoing changes, enabling a low-downtime cutover over the existing Direct Connect connection with minimal operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because installing MongoDB Community Edition on EC2 only creates another self-managed MongoDB cluster; it does not migrate data into the managed DocumentDB service and adds significant operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because AWS Data Pipeline is an orchestration service for batch data movement (primarily DynamoDB, RDS, S3, EMR) and is not designed to migrate a MongoDB replica set to DocumentDB with ongoing replication. Option D is incorrect because AWS Glue crawlers catalog data sources for ETL and analytics; they cannot act as a MongoDB source endpoint or perform continuous database replication to DocumentDB.

243.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Aurora MySQL provides the highest reliability of the listed databases: it replicates data six ways across three Availability Zones, supports fast automated failover to a replica, and offers self-healing storage. Running the stateless application tier in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer adds horizontal scalability and AZ-level fault tolerance, and storing session state in an ElastiCache for Redis replication group externalizes sessions in a highly available, failover-capable store so any instance can serve any user.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Neptune is a graph database and is not an appropriate session store. Option C is incorrect because DocumentDB is a document store unsuited to a relational MySQL workload, and Kinesis Data Firehose is a streaming delivery service, not a session store. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached does not support replication or persistence, so a node failure loses session data, making it less reliable than the Redis replication group in option B.

244.text:
Option C is correct because transit gateway route tables are the correct control point for restricting inter-VPC traffic at the network routing layer. By creating a dedicated transit gateway route table for each VPC attachment and adding routes only to the authorized VPCs, the solutions architect ensures each VPC can reach only its predefined set of peers. This is the native, scalable way to segment communication on a transit gateway and replaces the permissive default route table behavior that currently lets every VPC reach every other VPC.

Option A is incorrect because network ACLs are stateless and difficult to maintain at scale; managing per-subnet outbound rules for every VPC pairing is error-prone and operationally heavy. Option B is incorrect because security groups cannot reference security groups in other VPCs across a transit gateway, so this denial scheme is unworkable. Option D is incorrect because all VPC traffic already routes to the transit gateway by design; editing VPC route tables cannot enforce which other VPCs are reachable once traffic reaches the gateway's shared default route table.

245.text:
Options A, B, and D are correct because AWS Backup centrally orchestrates protection across EC2, EFS, and RDS with the least operational overhead. A single backup plan with a separate backup rule for each retention requirement satisfies the custom daily, weekly, and monthly lifecycle (option A). Configuring the plan to copy backups to another Region immediately after capture meets the cross-region replication requirement natively (option B). Adding an SNS topic to the backup plan and notifying on any job status except BACKUP_JOB_COMPLETED delivers immediate failure notifications, while the AWS Backup console provides the single source of backup status across the environment (option D).

Option C is incorrect because building a custom Lambda function to replicate backups and send failure notices reintroduces the operational overhead AWS Backup eliminates. Option E is incorrect because Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager only manages EBS snapshots and EBS-backed AMIs, so it cannot cover EFS or RDS or provide a unified status view. Option F is incorrect because manually configuring RDS snapshots fragments the solution and bypasses the centralized, single-pane management the requirements demand.

246.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) performs continuous block-level replication of the on-premises Windows servers, supporting an RPO measured in seconds and an RTO of minutes through automated launch of recovery instances, which satisfies the 15-minute RTO and 5-minute RPO. DRS provides native, automated failover and fallback to new or existing servers, and Amazon FSx for Windows File Server delivers the shared, fully managed SMB file system that the servers' common share requires, kept in sync with AWS DataSync.

Option A is incorrect because daily backups through a File Gateway yield an RPO of up to 24 hours and a slow recovery, far exceeding the targets. Option B is incorrect because rebuilding servers from CloudFormation and CodePipeline cannot achieve a 15-minute RTO, and EFS is Linux NFS, not a Windows SMB share. Option C is incorrect because a continuously running multi-site active-active environment is far more expensive than required and s3 sync does not replicate stateful Windows server workloads or provide native server failover and fallback.

247.text:
Option B is correct because the company lacks an inventory and needs detailed network connection and application dependency data, which the AWS Application Discovery Agent gathers by capturing running processes, performance metrics, and inbound/outbound network connections from each Windows and Linux server, physical or virtual. Feeding this data into AWS Migration Hub and running the Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations application data collector analyzes the discovered workloads and databases and generates a report with rightsizing and migration strategy guidance, giving the architect exactly the dependency-mapping detail needed to plan the migration.

Option A is incorrect because Migration Evaluator and the Agentless Collector focus on cost/business-case sizing and do not capture the detailed process-level application relationships and network connection data required here. Option C is incorrect because the Agentless Collector cannot capture application dependencies the way the agent does, and AWS Application Migration Service is for executing rehost migrations, not grouping servers for discovery reporting. Option D is incorrect because manually importing server details cannot reveal network connections or application relationships, which is precisely the unknown information the company needs to discover.

248.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement is near-real-time delivery of network connection data to an on-premises Splunk instance. Enabling VPC Flow Logs to CloudWatch captures which EC2 instances connect to which database endpoints, and a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter streams those log events continuously to a Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream. Firehose natively supports Splunk as a destination, and the pre-processing Lambda transformation extracts individual log events from the CloudWatch Logs records so Splunk receives properly formatted data in near real time with minimal management.

Option A is incorrect because periodically exporting CloudWatch logs to S3 and polling them is batch-oriented, not near-real-time, and embedding long-lived access keys in Splunk is poor security practice. Option C is incorrect because it relies on application-level request logging plus Athena queries and file-by-file Lambda pushes, which is slow, manual, and not near-real-time. Option D is incorrect because Kinesis Data Analytics anomaly detection solves a different problem; the requirement is simply to forward connection traffic data to Splunk, not to detect statistical anomalies.

249.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFormation cannot delete a non-empty S3 bucket, which is why the stack deletion failed. Implementing a Lambda-backed custom resource that empties the bucket (deletes all objects) on stack deletion solves the root cause without changing the application architecture. The custom resource's DependsOn attribute pointing to the bucket ensures the bucket is created before the custom resource and, during deletion, the custom resource runs to empty the bucket so CloudFormation can then remove it cleanly.

Option B is incorrect because migrating temporary storage to EFS is a major architectural change and does not address the immediate deletion problem. Option C is incorrect because an S3 Lifecycle rule only expires objects on a schedule (and 24-hour-lived objects may still exist at deletion time), so the bucket can still be non-empty when CloudFormation tries to delete it. Option D is incorrect because a DeletionPolicy of Delete is already the default behavior for S3 buckets and still fails when the bucket contains objects; it does not empty the bucket.

250.text:
Option D is correct because baking a new AMI that already includes the CodeDeploy agent and configuring the Auto Scaling group's launch template to use it ensures every newly launched instance is deployment-ready automatically. Critically, associating the CodeDeploy deployment group with the Auto Scaling group rather than with individual instances means CodeDeploy automatically deploys the current application revision to new instances as they scale out and registers them, eliminating the manual per-instance association entirely with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because building and maintaining an EventBridge-plus-Lambda mechanism to associate each new instance adds custom code and operational burden when native CodeDeploy/Auto Scaling integration already handles this. Option B is incorrect because suspending and resuming Auto Scaling around deployments and rebuilding AMIs is manual, slow, and fragile. Option C is incorrect because a full AMI-bake-and-instance-refresh pipeline through CodeBuild abandons the existing CodeDeploy/CodePipeline workflow and adds more moving parts than simply associating the deployment group with the Auto Scaling group.

251.text:
Option C is correct because once member accounts accept the AWS Organizations invitation, cross-account administration is achieved by creating the OrganizationAccountAccessRole IAM role in each member account and granting the management account permission to assume it. This trust relationship lets administrators in the centralized management account switch into each member account with the necessary permissions, providing the centralized access management the company needs alongside the consolidated billing that Organizations already delivers.

Option A is incorrect because an IAM group does not enable cross-account access; groups only organize users within a single account. Option B is incorrect because there is no OrganizationAccountAccessPolicy and a standalone IAM policy cannot establish the cross-account trust that role assumption provides. Option D is incorrect because the access role must exist in each member account (the account being accessed) so the management account can assume into it; creating it only in the management account and assigning it to member-account administrators inverts the trust direction and does not grant the management account control over the member accounts.

252.text:
Options A, C, and F are correct because together they remove the single points of failure causing the outages. Placing the Tomcat server in an Auto Scaling group with multiple EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer (option A) replaces the single web instance with a horizontally scalable, load-balanced tier that absorbs high traffic. Creating an additional public subnet in a second Availability Zone in the website VPC (option F) is required so the ALB and Auto Scaling group can span at least two AZs for fault tolerance. Migrating the MySQL database on a single EC2 instance to Amazon Aurora with a replica (option C) provides a managed, multi-AZ database with automated failover.

Option B is incorrect because adding another VPC peering connection does not improve reliability; a single peering connection is already redundant within AWS. Option D is incorrect because NAT gateways handle outbound internet access from private subnets and do nothing to address the website's traffic-driven outages. Option E is incorrect because moving the Tomcat server into the database VPC is an unnecessary topology change that does not add resilience.

253.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Storage Gateway File Gateway presents an NFS (and SMB) interface that maps directly to the company's NFS-based on-premises file storage, storing files as objects in S3. Because the company is willing to wait only a few days for disaster-recovery retrieval and wants the most cost-effective option, an S3 Lifecycle rule transitioning objects to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access after 5 days balances lower storage cost with immediate, no-fee retrieval suitable for these backup files.

Option B is incorrect because Volume Gateway presents iSCSI block volumes, not the NFS interface the company requires. Option C is incorrect because Tape Gateway presents a virtual tape library (iSCSI VTL) for backup applications, not an NFS file share. Option D is incorrect because, although File Gateway is right, transitioning to S3 Glacier Deep Archive forces multi-hour-to-day retrievals that exceed the company's stated tolerance more than necessary; the question's archive intent paired with NFS and cost balance makes Standard-IA the intended cost-effective fit while still meeting access needs.

254.text:
Option C is correct because hosting the 5 GB game file in Amazon S3 and distributing it through Amazon CloudFront gives worldwide users low-latency downloads from edge locations close to them, improving download performance regardless of location. CloudFront also reduces data transfer costs because origin-to-edge transfers are cheaper and cached content is served from the edge, and S3 provides durable, scalable storage without managing any FTP servers. Route 53 directs users to the distribution.

Option A is incorrect because running FTP on EC2 instances backed by EBS behind an ALB requires managing servers and scaling, lacks global edge caching, and incurs higher transfer cost and worse performance for distant users. Option B is incorrect for the same operational reasons, and EFS adds further cost without solving global distribution. Option D is incorrect because enabling Requester Pays shifts download charges to users and does not provide the global performance acceleration of CloudFront, failing the low-cost, high-performance worldwide-download goal.

255.text:
Option B is correct because the application logs show errors occurring specifically on calls to DynamoDB, and the symptom is increased response times under higher demand from the marketing campaign. This points to DynamoDB throttling provisioned read/write capacity. Implementing DynamoDB auto scaling lets the table automatically adjust provisioned RCUs and WCUs to match the increased and variable traffic, eliminating the capacity-related errors and restoring fast response times as the application grows in popularity.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda throttling accounts for only 1% of requests, so raising the concurrency limit addresses a minor symptom rather than the dominant DynamoDB-driven 10% error rate. Option C is incorrect because raising the API Gateway throttle limit would admit more traffic to an already-overloaded DynamoDB table, worsening the problem. Option D is incorrect because re-creating the table with a better-partitioned primary key is a major, disruptive change aimed at hot-partition issues; the evidence indicates insufficient provisioned throughput, which auto scaling solves directly with far less effort.

256.text:
Option A is correct because the gap is that the Application Load Balancer accepts unauthenticated requests while the API Gateway side already enforces a JWT authorizer. Application Load Balancers natively support built-in authentication and can integrate directly with an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider, requiring users to authenticate before requests are forwarded to backend targets. Configuring the ALB to authenticate against the existing OIDC IdP and allow only authenticated users closes the gap so that all backend services respond only to authenticated users.

Option B is incorrect because signed URLs control CloudFront object access and a permissive signing policy that allows any request explicitly defeats authentication. Option C is incorrect because AWS WAF filters on request attributes such as IP, headers, and rates but cannot perform an OIDC authentication handshake to distinguish authenticated from unauthenticated users. Option D is incorrect because CloudTrail logs management/API activity, not ALB request authentication, and a log-analysis-then-block approach is reactive, delayed, and does not prevent unauthenticated access in real time.

257.text:
Option B is correct because the tenant ID is available in each Lambda request, so logging the tenant ID along with the actual RCUs and WCUs consumed per transaction to CloudWatch Logs directly attributes DynamoDB usage to each tenant in a shared table. A scheduled Lambda function then computes per-tenant costs by combining those logged capacity units with the overall DynamoDB cost from the Cost Explorer API, and an EventBridge rule runs it on a schedule. This yields the granular per-tenant cost view with little ongoing effort because it uses consumption data the functions already handle.

Option A is incorrect because cost allocation tags on tables only break down cost per table, not per tenant in a shared multi-tenant table. Option C is incorrect because changing the partition key and using Athena to count items measures item counts, not actual consumed capacity, and is a disruptive data-model change. Option D is incorrect because response size and call duration do not map to DynamoDB capacity consumption, and the AWS Pricing Calculator estimates future costs rather than reporting actual incurred costs.

258.text:
Option B is correct because GitHub webhooks are the supported, low-latency mechanism to trigger an AWS CodePipeline pipeline when code is pushed. Using the Jenkins plugin for AWS CodeBuild lets the team keep their existing Jenkins-based unit testing within the pipeline, and sending alerts to an Amazon SNS topic notifies engineers of bad builds. A blue/green deployment with AWS CodeDeploy provisions a parallel replacement environment, enabling zero-downtime cutover and instant rollback by shifting traffic back to the original environment if a major issue arises.

Option A is incorrect because an in-place, all-at-once deployment causes downtime and lacks the seamless rollback the requirements demand, and GitHub websockets are not the integration mechanism. Option C is incorrect because GitHub websockets are not used to trigger CodePipeline, and AWS X-Ray is a distributed tracing tool, not a unit-testing or static-analysis service. Option D is incorrect for both the websockets/X-Ray misuse and the in-place, all-at-once deployment that violates the zero-downtime and rollback requirements.

259.text:
Option A is correct because the failure mode is connection exhaustion caused by many short-lived Lambda connections opening directly against the Aurora replicas during unpredictable traffic spikes. Amazon RDS Proxy pools and reuses database connections, drastically reducing the number of connections that reach the cluster and absorbing connection bursts. Configuring a read-only endpoint on the proxy routes the application's read-only operations across the reader instances, and pointing the Lambda function at the proxy endpoint improves reliability and resilience to spikes.

Option B is incorrect because raising max_connections plus rebooting is a manual, limited fix that does not solve connection churn from Lambda and still leaves the cluster vulnerable to bursts. Option C is incorrect because instance scaling reacts too slowly for unpredictable, short spikes and adds cost without addressing the root connection-management problem. Option D is incorrect because the Aurora Data API is a separate HTTP-based interface and is not configured as a read-only endpoint on RDS Proxy; the simple, correct solution is a standard RDS Proxy read-only endpoint as in option A.

260.text:
Option A is correct because the stack set is deploying into Regions that were previously unused, and a Region must be explicitly enabled (opted in) in the relevant accounts before CloudFormation can create stack instances there, which explains why no instances were created. Additionally, because the template creates an IAM role with a custom name, CloudFormation requires the CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM capability to acknowledge that named IAM resources are being created. Combining Region enablement with CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM allows the stacks to deploy successfully.

Option B is incorrect because the failure is not a stack quota issue, and CAPABILITY_IAM is insufficient when the IAM role has a custom name. Option C is incorrect because, although CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM is needed, choosing the SELF_MANAGED permissions model does not address the unenabled Regions and is not what makes the deployment succeed. Option D is incorrect because specifying an administration role ARN does not enable the new Regions, and CAPABILITY_IAM alone cannot create the custom-named IAM role.

261.text:
Option A is correct because the application cannot be modified or integrated with an identity provider, so authentication must be enforced at the load balancer. An Application Load Balancer supports built-in user authentication and integrates with Amazon Cognito: creating a Cognito user pool, populating it with the specific authorized users, requiring MFA on the pool, and configuring an ALB listener rule to authenticate through the Cognito hosted UI ensures only those users, after MFA, reach the Fargate service, all without changing the application code.

Option B is incorrect because IAM users authenticate to AWS APIs and the console, not to web traffic through an ALB, and you cannot attach a resource policy to a Fargate service to enforce end-user MFA. Option C is incorrect because IAM Identity Center provides workforce SSO to AWS and supported applications but does not offer an ALB "resource protection rule" to gate arbitrary web traffic. Option D is incorrect because AWS Amplify does not provide user pools or an ALB-integrated hosted-UI authentication listener; Amazon Cognito is the service that does.

262.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to store database credentials and supports automatic, scheduled rotation natively, including built-in rotation for Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL. Storing distinct secrets for the QA and production environments keeps credentials separated, turning on rotation satisfies the automatic password-rotation requirement, and referencing the secret from the Lambda functions (retrieved at runtime) keeps credentials out of application code, meeting both stated requirements with managed effort.

Option A is incorrect because, while Systems Manager Parameter Store can store encrypted credentials, it does not provide built-in automatic rotation; you would have to build and maintain rotation yourself. Option C is incorrect because AWS KMS manages encryption keys and does not store database credentials or rotate passwords for a database. Option D is incorrect because storing credentials in S3 objects, even with SSE-KMS, provides no automatic password rotation and is an insecure, operationally awkward pattern for managing database secrets.

263.text:
Option D is correct because node resilience for many stateless pods is maximized by spreading them across failure domains so that the loss of a single Availability Zone or node does not take down a disproportionate share of replicas. Kubernetes topology spread constraints based on Availability Zone instruct the scheduler to distribute pods evenly across AZs (and nodes), preventing concentration on one zone and ensuring the workload survives an AZ or node failure with minimal disruption as it scales replicas up and down.

Option A is incorrect because the EKS control plane is fully managed by AWS and already highly available across multiple AZs; deploying it into a second cluster is not how resilience is configured and is not a valid pattern. Option B is incorrect because fewer node groups with larger instances concentrates pods on fewer nodes, increasing blast radius and reducing resilience. Option C is incorrect because deliberately underprovisioning compute capacity starves pods of resources and harms reliability rather than improving node resilience.

264.text:
Option D is correct because the credentials are being committed to an AWS CodeCommit repository, so detection and remediation should be event-driven at commit time. Configuring a CodeCommit trigger to invoke a Lambda function on each new code submission scans the incoming code for credentials, and when found the function automatically disables the exposed IAM keys and notifies the user. This provides immediate, automatic remediation tied directly to the action that introduces the vulnerability.

Option A is incorrect because scanning development instances nightly does not inspect the CodeCommit repository where the secrets are actually committed, and it is slow and indirect. Option B is incorrect because a scheduled scan introduces delay between commit and detection, and storing newly generated credentials in AWS KMS is wrong since KMS manages encryption keys, not IAM access keys. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Macie discovers and classifies sensitive data in Amazon S3 and does not scan CodeCommit repositories, so it cannot detect the committed keys.

265.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS managed prefix list for CloudFront (com.amazonaws.global.cloudfront.origin-facing) is automatically maintained by AWS and contains the CloudFront origin-facing IP ranges. Referencing this managed prefix list in an ALB security group rule restricts inbound traffic to CloudFront only, so internet clients cannot reach the ALB directly. Because AWS keeps the prefix list current as CloudFront's IP ranges change, there is no ongoing maintenance, satisfying the least-operational-overhead requirement.

Option A is incorrect because duplicating the web ACL adds management burden and does not block direct network access to the ALB; WAF inspects requests but does not prevent the ALB from being reachable. Option B is incorrect for the same reason: associating a WAF web ACL filters requests but does not stop traffic from bypassing CloudFront and hitting the ALB directly. Option D is incorrect because manually entering CloudFront's many IP ranges is error-prone and the ranges change frequently, forcing constant manual updates and creating high operational overhead compared to the managed prefix list.

266.text:
Option B is correct because AWS IoT Greengrass extends AWS to edge devices and can run ML inference locally without internet connectivity. Deploying the trained SageMaker model to a Greengrass component on the local Linux server lets the company capture still images from the IP cameras, run inference at the edge, and call the local feedback API immediately. Because all processing happens on premises, defect feedback continues to work even when the factory's internet connection is down, meeting the offline requirement with minimal infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because streaming through Kinesis, EC2, S3, a SageMaker endpoint, and Lambda depends entirely on cloud connectivity, so feedback stops when the internet is down. Option C is incorrect because AWS Snowball is intended for bulk data transfer and edge compute that is ordered, shipped, and returned; it is not a permanent, low-overhead inference platform for an ongoing factory line. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Monitron is a purpose-built service for equipment vibration and temperature monitoring using its own sensors; it cannot run a custom SageMaker image-classification model or process IP camera images.

267.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses fronting endpoint groups in multiple AWS Regions, routing users to the optimal regional ALB over the AWS global network. Because Global Accelerator exposes static IPs, the company can create an A record (not a CNAME) for the apex (zone-apex) domain directly in its self-managed DNS, which is required since apex domains cannot use CNAME records. This gives multi-Region routing and health-based failover with the least effort while keeping DNS managed internally.

Option A is incorrect because CNAME records are not permitted at a zone apex per DNS standards, so pointing the apex domain at an ALB via CNAME is invalid, and it also forces migrating DNS to Route 53 unnecessarily. Option B is incorrect for the same apex-CNAME limitation, and ALBs do not expose static IPs that an NLB CNAME could neatly reference; it adds complexity. Option D is incorrect because building an API Gateway and Lambda round-robin router adds significant custom code, latency, and operational overhead and does not provide proper geographic routing.

268.text:
Option D is correct because a Lambda alias is a stable pointer to a specific function version. By configuring the client application to invoke the alias ARN once and then repointing the alias to each newly published version after testing, the company decouples the client from version changes. Updates become a single alias reconfiguration with no client redeployment and no invocation interruption, meeting the requirement to minimize disruption with the least operational overhead. Aliases also support weighted routing for gradual rollouts.

Option A is incorrect because published Lambda versions are immutable, so their environment variables cannot be modified, and testing on $LATEST does not solve the client-side ARN churn. Option B is incorrect because moving parameters to DynamoDB adds a new table, code changes, and per-invocation read latency and cost without addressing the core versioning problem. Option C is incorrect because hardcoding parameters still requires publishing a new version on every change and still forces the client to track new version ARNs, leaving the disruption unsolved.

269.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that natively integrates with S3 and is designed for exactly this transformation work. A Glue crawler with a custom classifier builds a table definition for the feed, and a Glue ETL job can mask the PAN, remove and merge fields, and output the records as JSON to the destination S3 bucket. Triggering the job with a Lambda on file delivery makes the pipeline event-driven, and adding future feeds is simply a matter of adding crawlers and jobs, satisfying the expandability requirement with low operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because chaining multiple Lambda functions through SQS with a temporary S3 staging area is fragile, complex to coordinate, and hard to expand. Option B is incorrect because managing Fargate scaling tied to queue depth adds operational complexity and is harder to extend than serverless Glue. Option D is incorrect because Athena cannot start an EMR ETL job, and standing up and scaling an EMR cluster introduces far more management effort than the serverless Glue approach.

270.text:
Option A is correct because provisioning an Amazon Aurora Replica in a different Region creates a cross-Region read replica that uses asynchronous physical replication managed entirely by Aurora. It enables fast promotion to a standalone writer for disaster recovery in the secondary Region with very low replication lag, and being a fully managed feature it requires minimal operational effort. Combined with the ECS Fargate tasks redeployed in the secondary Region, this meets the regulatory cross-Region recovery requirement while keeping data loss minimal.

Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync replicates files between storage locations such as NFS, SMB, S3, or EFS; it does not replicate a live relational Aurora database. Option C is incorrect because AWS DMS, while capable of continuous replication, adds replication instances and ongoing task management, which is far more operational overhead than the built-in Aurora cross-Region replica. Option D is incorrect because snapshots every 5 minutes still leave a recovery point of several minutes, risking data loss, and managing DLM snapshot copies across Regions adds overhead.

271.text:
Options A, C, and F are correct because cross-account access to a KMS-encrypted S3 bucket requires permissions on three layers. Option A adds a bucket policy in the Creative account granting read access with the Strategy account ID as the principal, allowing the cross-account identity to be authorized at the resource. Option F grants the strategy_reviewer role read permissions on the bucket and decrypt permissions on the KMS key, satisfying the identity-side policy with least privilege. Option C updates the custom KMS key policy to grant the role kms:Decrypt, which is mandatory because the key policy must explicitly allow the external principal to use the key to decrypt objects.

Option B is incorrect because granting full S3 permissions violates the least-privilege requirement when only read access is needed. Option D is incorrect because setting the bucket policy principal to an anonymous user would expose the bucket publicly, which is insecure. Option E is incorrect because the team only needs to view (read) objects, so decrypt is required but encrypt permission is unnecessary and exceeds the minimum needed.

272.text:
Options C and E are correct because Service Control Policies in AWS Organizations centrally enforce guardrails across accounts with minimal ongoing maintenance. Option C creates an SCP using the aws:RequestedRegion condition key to deny actions in every Region except ap-northeast-1 and attaches it to the root OU, enforcing the regulatory Region restriction organization-wide. Option E creates a separate SCP using the ec2:InstanceType condition key to restrict EC2 launches to the predefined instance types and attaches it only to the DataOps OU, applying the instance-type rule precisely where required.

Option A is incorrect because an IAM role in a single account does not scale or enforce the rule across all DataOps accounts and adds per-account maintenance. Option B is incorrect because creating IAM users in every account with inline policies is high-maintenance and does not provide an organization-wide guardrail; SCPs are the right tool. Option D is incorrect because ec2:Region is not a valid condition key for Region restriction; aws:RequestedRegion is the correct global condition key, so the SCP would not work as intended.

273.text:
Option A is correct because an AWS Storage Gateway file gateway presents an SMB (or NFS) file share that the on-premises SQL Server can write its nightly export to, and the gateway then stores those files natively as objects directly in Amazon S3. This delivers durable, low-cost S3 storage for the backups while transferring data efficiently over the existing 10 Gbps Direct Connect link. Because the data lands directly in S3 with no extra managed file system to run, this is the most cost-effective option.

Option B and Option C are incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provisions a full managed Windows file system that is more expensive than S3 object storage for simple backup archival, and the requirement is to move backups to S3; Multi-AZ in option C adds even more cost. Option D is incorrect because a volume gateway presents iSCSI block volumes, not SMB file shares as the option states, and stores point-in-time EBS snapshots rather than writing the backup files directly into S3, adding cost and complexity.

274.text:
Options A, C, and E are correct because ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded indicates consumers are exceeding the per-shard read throughput limits of Kinesis Data Streams. Option A reshards to increase the number of shards, raising total read capacity and spreading load. Option C uses enhanced fan-out, which gives each registered consumer its own dedicated 2 MB/s per-shard read pipe via HTTP/2 push, eliminating contention among multiple applications reading the same stream. Option E adds error retries with exponential backoff in consumer logic so transient throttling is handled gracefully and retried without overwhelming the stream.

Option B is incorrect because the Kinesis Producer Library is a producer-side optimization and adjusting polling frequency does not resolve read-throughput limits across multiple consumers. Option D is incorrect because reducing the number of shards lowers read capacity and would worsen the throttling. Option F is incorrect because dynamic partitioning is a Kinesis Data Firehose feature for partitioning delivery to S3, not a mechanism for resolving Kinesis Data Streams consumer read throttling.

275.text:
Option C is correct because using Auto Scaling groups for the backend EC2 services lets the fleet scale out automatically during peak seasonal traffic and scale in afterward, and enabling DynamoDB auto scaling automatically adjusts provisioned read and write capacity to match demand. This combination directly addresses the slow read and write performance during peaks using built-in AWS scaling features, requiring the least development effort since no application rewrite is needed.

Option A is incorrect because migrating the backend to Lambda is a significant re-architecture effort, and statically increasing DynamoDB capacity does not adapt to fluctuating seasonal load. Option B is also a major rewrite to Lambda, and DynamoDB global tables provide multi-Region replication for availability, not the scaling of read/write throughput needed here. Option D requires re-architecting to a queue-and-Lambda write path, adding development effort and asynchronous complexity that is unnecessary when DynamoDB auto scaling solves the throughput problem more simply.

276.text:
