Option A is correct because tag policies in AWS Organizations must be created in the organization's management account, where they define the allowed (compliant) values for the project tag and report noncompliant resources across the organization. Pairing this with an SCP that denies cloudformation:CreateStack unless a project tag is present, attached to each OU, enforces that new infrastructure deployed through CloudFormation carries the required tag. This combination uses native Organizations features to standardize and enforce tagging with the least effort.

Option B is incorrect because tag policies are created and managed centrally in the management account, not separately in each OU, which would add duplication and maintenance. Option C is incorrect because an IAM policy assigned to each user is high-maintenance, does not scale across the organization, and relies on per-user assignment rather than organization-wide enforcement. Option D is incorrect because using Service Catalog with TagOptions requires building and maintaining products and a portfolio, which is far more effort than applying a tag policy and SCP, and it only governs resources launched through the catalog.

277.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk implements blue/green deployments through the Swap Environment URLs action. After deploying the new version to an identical (green) environment and validating it, selecting Swap Environment URLs swaps the CNAMEs of the two environments, instantly redirecting production traffic to the new environment with no downtime. Elastic Beanstalk handles the DNS swap natively, making this the correct and intended final step.

Option A is incorrect because manually redirecting with Route 53 bypasses the built-in Elastic Beanstalk swap mechanism and is not how Beanstalk blue/green deployments are completed. Option C is incorrect because replacing the Auto Scaling launch configuration modifies how instances are provisioned and does not perform the traffic cutover between environments. Option D is incorrect because manually updating DNS records to point to the green environment duplicates what the Swap Environment URLs feature does automatically and risks misconfiguration; the native swap is the proper approach.

278.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Connect is a fully managed, serverless cloud contact center that replaces the aging on-premises call center hardware while improving reliability with AWS-managed infrastructure. Amazon Pinpoint provides managed, two-way interactive SMS messaging, which is exactly what is needed to send and receive the interactive survey by text message. Together they deliver the calling and the two-way survey capability with the least ongoing operational overhead because both are fully managed services.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon SNS sends one-way notification messages and does not support the managed two-way interactive survey experience that the requirement describes; Pinpoint is the correct service for that. Option C is incorrect because running the call center software on EC2 Auto Scaling instances keeps significant operational overhead for patching, scaling, and maintaining the application. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Pinpoint is a customer engagement and messaging service and cannot replace a call center's voice/telephony function, which is the role of Amazon Connect.

279.text:
Option C is correct because the browser enforces CORS on the actual API call, which targets the Amazon API Gateway endpoint, not the CloudFront/S3 origin serving the static page. Enabling CORS on the API Gateway resource and ensuring its responses (including the preflight OPTIONS method and the integration/error responses) return the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header set to www.example.com lets the browser permit the cross-origin request. Since the failing request is to API Gateway, configuring CORS there resolves the error.

Option A is incorrect because S3 CORS settings govern requests made directly to the S3 bucket, but the registration form calls the API Gateway endpoint, not S3, so changing S3 CORS does not address the error. Option B is incorrect because AWS WAF inspects and filters requests based on rules; it has no CORS configuration and does not inject CORS response headers. Option D is incorrect because while a Lambda proxy integration can return CORS headers, there is no separate CORS setting to enable on the Lambda function itself, and the correct, complete place to configure CORS (including preflight) is the API Gateway API.

280.text:
Options B and D are correct because the failures stem from DynamoDB write performance and Lambda concurrency limits during traffic spikes. Option B evaluates and adjusts the write capacity units (WCUs), since the described bottleneck is the performance when data is saved (written) to DynamoDB, directly addressing throttled writes. Option D inserts an Amazon SQS queue with reprocessing logic between S3 and Lambda, which buffers the flood of upload events, smooths the load against Lambda concurrency limits, and lets failed processing be retried so no photos are dropped, improving reliability.

Option A is incorrect because adjusting read capacity units does not help when the problem is the performance of saving (writing) data. Option C is incorrect because ElastiCache is a caching layer for reads and does not raise Lambda concurrency or improve write durability. Option E is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration only speeds up uploads from distant users; it does nothing to address Lambda concurrency limits or DynamoDB write throttling, which are the actual causes of the processing failures.

281.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Cost Explorer provides built-in rightsizing recommendations that identify underutilized EC2 instances and suggest downsizing options, and these recommendations can be retrieved organization-wide from the management (payer) account. Installing the CloudWatch agent via AWS Systems Manager supplies the memory utilization data that Cost Explorer needs in addition to CPU. Centralizing retrieval in the management account covers all member accounts at once, meeting the requirement with the least effort.

Option A is incorrect because installing a third-party Marketplace tool and writing a custom Python script with manual pricing lookups is far more effort than using the native Cost Explorer recommendations. Option C is incorrect because retrieving recommendations from each individual account is unnecessary work; the management account aggregates rightsizing recommendations across the organization. Option D is incorrect because building a Lambda extraction pipeline with S3 storage and Athena queries plus manual pricing references reinvents functionality that Cost Explorer already provides, greatly increasing effort.

282.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS security best practice for granting external parties access is cross-account IAM role assumption. Creating an IAM role in the company's account that trusts the auditors' AWS account, attaching a policy with only read-only permissions, and adding a unique external ID to the trust policy lets the auditors assume the role using their own credentials. The external ID protects against the confused-deputy problem, and no long-term credentials are shared, providing secure, temporary, read-only access.

Option A is incorrect because creating individual resource policies on every resource is unmanageable and error-prone, and external IDs apply to IAM role trust policies, not resource policies. Option C is incorrect because creating an IAM user and sharing long-lived access keys with a third party violates security best practices, since static credentials can be leaked and are hard to rotate. Option D is incorrect because creating IAM users inside the company's account for each external auditor manages third-party identities directly, which contradicts best practice; cross-account role assumption with the auditors' own account is preferred.

283.text:
Options A, C, and F are correct because tightly coupled HPC performance depends on low-latency networking and a high-throughput shared file system. Option A launches the cluster within a single Availability Zone (ideally in a cluster placement group) to minimize inter-node network latency for the tightly coupled workload. Option C selects instance types with an Elastic Fabric Adapter, which provides the low-latency, high-throughput OS-bypass networking that HPC and MPI workloads need to scale. Option F replaces Amazon EFS with Amazon FSx for Lustre, a high-performance parallel file system purpose-built for HPC that delivers far greater throughput and IOPS for large shared datasets than EFS, removing the bottleneck seen at 1,000 instances.

Option B is incorrect because attaching network interfaces in multiples of four does not improve HPC interconnect performance. Option D is incorrect because spreading the cluster across multiple Availability Zones increases inter-node latency, harming tightly coupled performance. Option E is incorrect because per-instance EBS RAID arrays are local block storage and do not provide the shared, parallel file system the cluster requires.

284.text:
Option B is correct because the gaming instances run all year and must always be available, which makes an EC2 Instance Savings Plan ideal: it provides substantial discounts over On-Demand in exchange for a committed, steady usage for the continuously running fleet. The analytics application can be interrupted and resumed, which is the exact profile Spot Instances are designed for, offering the deepest discounts for fault-tolerant, interruption-tolerant workloads. This pairing minimizes cost for both workloads.

Option A is incorrect because running the interruptible analytics application on On-Demand Instances pays full price for a workload that could use much cheaper Spot Instances. Option C is incorrect because running the always-on gaming application on Spot Instances risks interruption of a workload that must always be available, and Service Catalog does not provide compute discounts. Option D is incorrect because using On-Demand for the always-on gaming fleet forgoes the Savings Plan discount it qualifies for, and Service Catalog again does not reduce instance pricing.

285.text:
Option D is correct because associating the specific member accounts with a dedicated OU lets the company apply governance precisely to just those accounts. Attaching an SCP that uses condition keys (such as aws:RequestedRegion) to that OU limits the Regions where resources can be deployed, and attaching a tag policy enforces the group tagging standard. This is centrally managed through AWS Organizations with minimal configuration and targets only the intended accounts.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config rules detect and report noncompliance but do not preventively restrict Regions, and per-account configuration is not centrally managed with minimal effort. Option B is incorrect because disabling Regions in the Billing console is an account-level setting not driven per member account from billing, and it does not enforce tagging standards. Option C is incorrect because attaching policies at the root would apply the Region restriction to all accounts in the organization rather than only the specific member accounts; placing them in a dedicated OU scopes the controls correctly.

286.text:
Options B, C, and E are correct because they together provide intra-Region high availability, sub-minute cross-Region failover, and an efficient multi-Region data layer. Option B uses a Route 53 failover routing policy from the primary to the disaster recovery Region with a 30-second TTL, enabling DNS to redirect users quickly (within about a minute) after a health check failure. Option C uses an Amazon DynamoDB global table so the NoSQL data is actively replicated and readable in both Regions, supporting fast failover without restore steps. Option E implements a hot standby with Auto Scaling groups across multiple Availability Zones for the web and application tiers, using Reserved Instances for the baseline and On-Demand for bursts, giving HA within a Region and a ready-to-serve standby.

Option A is incorrect because a 100/0 weighted policy with a 1-hour TTL would not fail over within 1 minute due to DNS caching. Option D is incorrect because 60-minute backups plus S3 replication and a manual import script cause data loss and slow recovery, not sub-minute failover. Option F is incorrect because Spot Instances can be interrupted, making them unsuitable for the always-available standby tiers.

287.text:
Option B is correct because making the API Gateway endpoint type private and creating an interface VPC endpoint (powered by AWS PrivateLink) keeps all traffic between the EC2 microservices and the API on the AWS private network, never traversing the public internet. Adding an endpoint policy that allows access only to the specific API, plus an API Gateway resource policy that allows access only from the VPC endpoint, locks down access so the API is reachable only privately from within the VPC. This precisely meets both requirements.

Option A is incorrect because a Site-to-Site VPN connects on-premises networks to a VPC, not a VPC to API Gateway, and API keys identify callers but do not make the API private. Option C is incorrect because API Gateway is a managed service that cannot be moved into a customer VPC, and IAM authentication does not by itself keep traffic off the public internet. Option D is incorrect because Global Accelerator exposes public static IPs over the internet edge and does not make the API private; routing VPC subnets to it does not satisfy the private-access requirement.

288.text:
Option B is correct because the firewall appliance needs a stable set of IP addresses to allow-list, and Network Load Balancers support assigning static (or Elastic) IP addresses per Availability Zone, which ALBs do not natively provide. Creating an NLB with static IPs in multiple AZs and configuring an ALB-type target group so the NLB forwards to the existing ALB preserves the ALB's TLS termination and path-based routing while exposing the fixed IPs that the firewall can allow-list. Clients then connect to the NLB, satisfying the IP allow-list requirement without losing layer 7 features.

Option A is incorrect because Application Load Balancers do not support assigning static IP addresses, so the firewall cannot reliably allow-list them. Option C is incorrect because adding the existing target groups directly to the NLB and deleting the ALB loses the ALB's path-based (layer 7) routing and TLS termination that the application requires. Option D is incorrect because a Gateway Load Balancer is for deploying third-party virtual appliances using the GENEVE protocol, not for fronting an ALB to provide static client-facing IPs.

289.text:
Options A, C, and E are correct because they form the complete identity-federation and centralized-management solution. Option A creates a management account and an AWS Organizations structure, inviting the existing accounts to join, which delivers consolidated billing and centralized governance with no rebuilding of accounts. Option C deploys IAM Identity Center in the management account and connects it to the existing Azure Active Directory as the identity source, with SCIM automatic synchronization of users and groups, so the company federates identities instead of managing users manually. Option E creates permission sets and attaches them to Identity Center groups and accounts; permission sets are delivered as IAM roles assumed via federation, which issues short-lived temporary credentials rather than long-lived access keys.

Option B only standardizes account email addresses and does nothing for federation, temporary credentials, or organization management. Option D and Option F propose AWS Managed Microsoft AD shared via RAM and per-account IAM authentication against it; this duplicates the directory the company already has in Azure AD, adds significant operational overhead, and does not natively provide the temporary-credential, single-sign-on federation that IAM Identity Center delivers out of the box.

290.text:
Option D is correct because the query time grows as Athena scans an ever-larger volume of accumulated log data in S3. Partitioning the data by date and time in both the Kinesis Data Firehose delivery configuration and the Athena table definition lets Athena prune partitions so each daily query reads only the previous 24 hours of objects. This keeps the bytes scanned constant regardless of total history, holding both query time and cost steady with minimal ongoing management since Firehose handles the partitioned write paths automatically.

Option A consolidates files but still scans the full accumulated dataset, so query time keeps rising, and it adds a custom Lambda to maintain. Option B sends logs to a new bucket each day, but that forces the query to change targets daily and breaks a single consistent table, adding operational overhead without a clean partition model. Option C introduces Amazon Redshift Spectrum and external tables, replacing the existing Athena solution with a more complex service stack and additional infrastructure, which contradicts the requirement to minimize operational overhead.

291.text:
Option B is correct because creating a second target group on the same ALB and using weighted target groups lets the listener forward a precise percentage of traffic (10%) to the new business logic while the rest continues to the existing version. Enabling target group stickiness ensures that once a customer is routed to a target group, subsequent requests during the testing window keep going to the same version, satisfying the requirement that a customer use one consistent version throughout the test. This is the simplest canary mechanism and reuses the existing ALB and Route 53 record.

Option A adds a second ALB and uses Route 53 weighted DNS, which is coarse, subject to DNS caching, and does not reliably pin an individual customer to one version. Option C uses an AutoScalingRollingUpdate with MaxBatchSize, which replaces instances in batches rather than splitting customer traffic by percentage, so all customers eventually receive the new logic. Option D relies on least-outstanding-requests routing across two Auto Scaling groups, which does not control the traffic split percentage and cannot guarantee the 10% allocation.

292.text:
Options B, D, and E are correct because together they replace the on-premises stack with fully managed equivalents. Option B has the IoT devices publish over MQTT to AWS IoT Core, a managed service that handles device connectivity and message ingestion without running a connection server. Option D writes the device metadata to Amazon DocumentDB, which is MongoDB-compatible and fully managed, so the company keeps its data model while eliminating cluster administration. Option E uses AWS Step Functions orchestrating Lambda tasks to run the 120-600 second aggregation jobs serverlessly and write reports to S3, then serves them through CloudFront, removing the always-on report server.

Option A is unnecessary because IoT Core, not Lambda, manages device connections; devices publish directly. Option C keeps a self-managed MongoDB on EC2, which preserves the patching and scaling burden the company is trying to shed. Option F runs reports on an EKS cluster backed by EC2 instances with an ingress controller, which carries substantial cluster operational overhead compared to the serverless Step Functions and CloudFront approach.

293.text:
Options A, B, and E are correct because they deliver bidirectional, automatic synchronization with minimal management. Option B configures two-way S3 Cross-Region Replication so objects written to either bucket are automatically replicated to the other, keeping both Regions consistent without custom code. Option E enables S3 Versioning on each bucket, which is a hard prerequisite for CRR and also helps reconcile concurrent writes in an active-active design. Option A creates an S3 Multi-Region Access Point and points the application at it, giving a single global endpoint that routes requests to the lowest-latency bucket while both Regions operate simultaneously.

Option C requires the application to write to both buckets itself, pushing replication logic and failure handling into application code instead of using managed replication. Option D misuses S3 Lifecycle rules, which transition or expire objects within a bucket and cannot copy objects between buckets. Option F builds a custom Lambda-and-event-notification copy pipeline, which adds code, error-handling, and operational overhead that native CRR already handles automatically.

294.text:
Option C is correct because AWS WAF includes an AWS Managed Rules SQL database rule group specifically designed to detect and block SQL injection patterns in HTTP requests. Creating a web ACL with a rule that blocks requests matching this SQLi rule group, while allowing all other traffic by default, stops the malicious requests at the ALB before they reach the ECS tasks. Attaching the web ACL to the ALB filters the attack inline with no custom code, which maximizes operational efficiency and lets legitimate traffic pass through.

Protection requires the rule action to be Block; Option A only sets the web ACL to monitor (Count) requests, so it detects but never stops the attacks. Option B uses AWS WAF Bot Control, which targets automated bot traffic, not SQL injection, and again only monitors. Option D builds a custom Lambda that scrapes logs to populate an IP set reactively, which is slow, lags behind active attacks, and adds significant operational overhead compared to the purpose-built managed SQLi rule group.

295.text:
Option D is correct because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 and supports the HTTPS-based ECS services and the dynamic host port mapping needed for blue/green deployments through CodeDeploy. Pairing it with ECS Service Auto Scaling lets the service automatically adjust the desired task count in response to CloudWatch alarms, which scales from 5 million to 30 million users without managing servers on Fargate. This combination is the native, managed pattern for HTTP/HTTPS microservices that require traffic-shifted blue/green releases.

Options A and B use a Network Load Balancer, which is Layer 4 and less suited to HTTP-based services and listener-rule traffic shifting; Option A also just raises a service quota rather than implementing automatic scaling at all. Options B and C both reference the Cluster Autoscaler with Auto Scaling groups, which is an Amazon EKS / EC2 capacity concept and is not how ECS on Fargate scales tasks. Only Service Auto Scaling adjusts ECS task counts, making Option D the correct choice.

296.text:
Option B is correct because configuring the EC2 instances to ship application logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs using the unified CloudWatch agent streams the log data off the instance in near real time. Because the logs are persisted centrally in CloudWatch Logs, they remain available and searchable even after an Auto Scaling scale-in event terminates the instance that generated them, giving the development team full access to historical application logs for performance analysis.

Option A enables ALB access logs, which capture load balancer request metadata but not the application's internal logs that the team needs to analyze performance. Option C changes the scaling policy to step scaling, which only alters how the group scales and does nothing to preserve logs. Option D adds AWS X-Ray tracing, which provides distributed request traces and latency insights but is not a substitute for retaining the application log files, so it does not meet the stated requirement.

297.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Application Migration Service performs a lift-and-shift rehost of the existing application server to EC2, which is the fastest path to AWS and accelerates the migration without rewriting the application. Placing the instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer resolves the overutilization and upload failures by distributing load and scaling out. Refactoring the app to persist files in Amazon S3 removes the need to keep adding storage to a file server, and Amazon Cognito provides managed authentication so only authenticated users can upload files.

Option B is nearly identical but uses IAM Identity Center for end-user sign-in; Identity Center is intended for workforce access to AWS, not for authenticating external application users, which Cognito handles. Options C and D rebuild the application as a static site with AppSync or Amplify; while modern, these require a substantially larger rewrite of the existing server-based application, increasing development effort rather than minimizing it, so they do not meet the LEAST operational overhead and rapid-migration goals as well as Option A.

298.text:
Option A is correct because the customers have hard-coded the existing SFTP endpoint IP addresses into their firewall allow lists and those addresses cannot change. By using Bring Your Own IP to register the company's owned address block in its AWS account and creating Elastic IP addresses from that pool, the company can assign the same static IPs to an AWS Transfer Family SFTP endpoint. AWS Transfer Family is a fully managed SFTP service that stores files directly in Amazon S3, eliminating server administration and meeting the requirement to decrease operational overhead while preserving the unchangeable IP addresses.

Option B and Option C build self-managed FTP servers on EC2 in Auto Scaling groups, which reintroduces patching, scaling, and server management overhead and uses plain FTP rather than SFTP. Option C also incorrectly attempts to register IP addresses with Route 53, which is DNS, not IP allocation. Option D assigns Elastic IPs to an S3 VPC endpoint and claims native SFTP support on the bucket, but S3 does not provide SFTP directly and VPC endpoints are not assigned customer Elastic IPs, so it is technically invalid.

299.text:
Option B is correct because the threat is a high-volume burst of failed login attempts from roughly 500 IP addresses that change every week. An AWS WAF web ACL with a rate-based rule set to Block automatically tracks request rates per source IP and blocks any client that exceeds the threshold, then releases it when the rate drops. Because it keys on request rate rather than specific addresses, it adapts to the changing IPs each week with no manual updates, and attaching it to the ALB filters traffic before it reaches the authentication service. This is the most operationally efficient defense.

Options A and C use AWS Firewall Manager to manage security groups; security groups cannot rate-limit and would require constantly editing rules as the IPs change, which is high overhead and impractical. Option D uses a WAF IP set match rule, which blocks only a fixed list of addresses; since the offending IPs rotate weekly, the administrator would have to manually update the IP set continuously, defeating the operational-efficiency requirement.

300.text:
Option B is correct because IAM Access Analyzer continuously evaluates resource policies and generates a finding whenever an S3 bucket grants access outside the account, including public access, reporting an isPublic indicator. Creating an analyzer and an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches the Access Analyzer Finding event type filtered for isPublic: true, with the existing SNS topic as the target, sends the data security team an email only when a bucket actually becomes publicly exposed. This is a purpose-built, low-overhead detection mechanism for exactly this condition.

Option A invents an isPublic S3 event notification; S3 event notifications fire on object operations like creation or deletion, not on a bucket becoming public, so no such event exists. Option C alerts on every PutBucketPolicy call, which fires for many policy changes that do not result in public exposure, producing noisy false positives. Option D uses an unrelated AWS Config rule (cloudtrail-s3-dataevents-enabled) that checks whether data-event logging is on, not whether a bucket is public, so it would not detect the required condition.

301.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon S3 provides durable, virtually unlimited object storage for the 10,000 concurrent uploads, and configuring an S3 event notification to an Amazon SQS queue decouples ingestion from processing. A fleet of EC2 worker instances pulls messages from the queue to overlay text and writes the processed images to a second S3 bucket, and scaling the fleet on CloudWatch queue-depth metrics matches compute to the backlog so no work is lost during spikes. Serving the processed bucket through Amazon CloudFront delivers images globally with low latency.

Option A stores images on EFS and drives processing off CloudWatch Logs, an awkward and tightly coupled design with a single-instance CloudFront origin. Option B sends notifications to SNS, but SNS is push/pub-sub and does not durably buffer work for pull-based workers the way SQS does, risking lost messages if instances are busy. Option D relies on a shared EBS volume across many instances, which EBS does not support as standard multi-attach for general fleets, and adds a DynamoDB tracking table that increases complexity unnecessarily.

302.text:
Option B is correct because the on-premises company.example DNS zone is private and reachable only over the Site-to-Site VPN. Turning on DNS hostnames for the VPC and creating an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint with a forwarding rule for company.example directs the VPC's DNS queries for that domain to the on-premises name servers across the VPN. This lets the new EC2 service resolve on-premises hostnames using a fully managed resolver, integrating cleanly with the existing services.

Option A creates an empty Route 53 private zone and tries to delegate via an NS record, but the authoritative data lives on premises and is not in Route 53, so resolution would fail. Option C configures an inbound resolver endpoint, which handles DNS queries flowing from on premises into AWS, the opposite direction of what is needed here; the VPC needs to forward outbound to on-premises servers. Option D installs static hosts files via Systems Manager on instance launch, a brittle, high-maintenance approach that breaks whenever on-premises hostnames or IPs change.

303.text:
Option C is correct because denying operations outside designated Regions across hundreds of accounts is best enforced with Service Control Policies in AWS Organizations. Launching an AWS Control Tower landing zone establishes the multi-account organization with a baseline of governance and organizational units, and attaching SCPs that deny actions outside the approved Regions applies a guardrail that even account administrators and root users cannot override. This scales automatically to new accounts placed in the OUs with the least operational overhead.

Option A relies on IAM policies and roles managed per account, which does not scale to hundreds of accounts and can be circumvented or inconsistently applied. Option B attaches policies to IAM users in each account, but IAM identity policies are not a centralized organization-wide guardrail and per-user management is unmanageable at this scale; SCPs, not user policies, enforce Region restrictions across accounts. Option D uses Security Hub, which assesses and reports on security posture but does not preventively deny actions in specific Regions, so it cannot enforce the requirement.

304.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Backup is a fully managed, centralized backup service that supports Amazon RDS. Turning on its cross-account management feature lets the organization manage and monitor backups from a single management account. A backup plan can specify a 6-hour frequency and 30-day retention, and applying the plan by resource tags automatically includes the tagged DB instances. AWS Backup also provides a consolidated dashboard to monitor backup job status across accounts, satisfying the consolidated-health-view requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option B describes an RDS cross-account management feature and a snapshot global policy that do not exist as native RDS capabilities. Option C builds a CloudFormation StackSet plus a custom Lambda and per-account EventBridge rules to monitor status, which adds substantial custom infrastructure and overhead compared to AWS Backup's built-in monitoring. Option D uses Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager configured per account; DLM manages EBS snapshots and AMIs, not RDS DB instance backups, and the per-account console monitoring fails to provide the required consolidated cross-account view.

305.text:
Option A is correct because a private Amazon ECR repository keeps the Docker images internal, and a repository permissions policy that allows the required ECR operations with a condition on the aws:PrincipalOrgID condition key grants access to every account in the organization automatically. Using aws:PrincipalOrgID means newly created accounts in the organization gain access without policy edits, scaling cleanly to hundreds of accounts. An ECR lifecycle rule that deletes untagged images beyond a count of five retains all tagged images while automatically pruning old untagged ones, all natively with no custom code.

Options B and D create a public repository, which would expose the images to anyone, violating the requirement that only organization accounts have access. Options C and D replace the simple, native ECR lifecycle rule with a scheduled EventBridge rule and a custom Lambda function to delete untagged images, adding ongoing operational overhead. Option C also enumerates account IDs rather than using aws:PrincipalOrgID, which would require constant updates as accounts are added.

306.text:
Option B is correct because the nightly statistical job is non-critical and tolerant of failure, since data points simply roll into the next run, which makes it an ideal fit for Amazon EC2 Spot Instances and their large cost savings. Decoupling ingestion by using Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to write the streaming data to Amazon S3 removes the always-on Reserved Instance fleet and its attached EBS volumes, replacing them with a managed, pay-as-you-go ingestion path. Running the 4-hour batch with AWS Batch on Spot Instances provisions and tears down compute automatically at the lowest cost, making this the most cost-effective design.

Option A uses On-Demand Instances for the nightly batch, which is far more expensive than Spot for a fault-tolerant workload. Option C keeps a full-time fleet of 3-year Reserved Instances behind an NLB for ingestion, retaining costly always-on capacity instead of serverless Firehose ingestion. Option D streams into Amazon Redshift and runs a Lambda nightly, but a 4-hour aggregation exceeds Lambda's 15-minute maximum execution time and an always-on Redshift cluster adds cost, so it neither fits nor minimizes spend.

307.text:
Option C is correct because making comments appear in real time requires the server to push updates to clients as they happen rather than having clients repeatedly ask for data. AWS AppSync supports GraphQL subscriptions over WebSockets, maintaining a persistent connection that delivers new comments to subscribed users the moment they are posted. This eliminates polling latency and provides a managed, scalable real-time experience that directly reduces comment latency and improves engagement.

Option A uses an edge-optimized API with CloudFront caching, which speeds up repeated reads of static responses but actually works against real-time data because cached responses become stale and it does not push updates. Option B polls GET /comments every 10 seconds, which introduces up to 10 seconds of latency, wastes requests, and scales poorly. Option D adjusts Lambda concurrency limits, which can affect throughput but does nothing to deliver comments in real time, since the client would still need to request them; only a push mechanism like AppSync WebSockets meets the requirement.

308.text:
Option C is correct because IPv6 addresses on AWS are globally routable, so there is no NAT for IPv6 and instances in private subnets must use an egress-only internet gateway to reach the internet for outbound connections while blocking all inbound connections initiated from the internet. Associating an Amazon-provided IPv6 CIDR block with the VPC and subnets enables IPv6, and routing ::/0 from the private subnets to the egress-only internet gateway gives those instances outbound IPv6 access without making them publicly reachable, exactly meeting the requirement.

Option A routes ::/0 to the regular internet gateway, which allows inbound connectivity and would make the private instances reachable from the internet. Option B routes IPv6 traffic to a NAT gateway, but NAT gateways only support IPv4 and cannot route IPv6. Option D invents an IPv6-enabled NAT gateway; NAT gateways do not support IPv6, and the correct construct for outbound-only IPv6 is the egress-only internet gateway, so Option C is the only valid solution.

309.text:
Option D is correct because the legitimate users are six known partners who each call the API once per day, while the unwanted traffic is high-volume requests from 500 changing IPs. Creating an AWS WAF web ACL with a rule that allows only the partners' known IP addresses and associating it with the API restricts access at the network edge. Adding an API Gateway usage plan with a request limit and an API key, then distributing the key to the partners, enforces per-key throttling and quotas so even allowed clients cannot exceed expected volume. This combination secures the API while minimizing cost.

Options A and B place CloudFront in front and try to block clients exceeding five requests per day, but WAF rate-based rules operate on a rolling time window, not a fixed daily count, so the per-day logic is not natively supported and the extra CloudFront layer adds cost. Option C uses a resource policy with a request limit and an API key, but API Gateway enforces request limits and keys through usage plans, not resource policies, making Option D the technically correct approach.

310.text:
Option B is correct because the company already stores the curated data in an Amazon Redshift cluster, and AWS Data Exchange supports creating a datashare directly from Redshift. Connecting AWS Data Exchange to the Redshift cluster lets subscribers query the live data, so customers automatically access the most recent published data without any file export step. Configuring subscription verification confirms each customer's identity before access is granted, and requiring customers to subscribe to the data product manages entitlements centrally, all with the least operational overhead since it eliminates the FTP file-distribution process.

Option A builds an API Gateway Data API integration and uses AWS Data Exchange for APIs, adding a custom API layer and more moving parts than a native Redshift datashare. Option C periodically downloads data to S3 and shares via AWS Data Exchange for S3, which reintroduces a copy/export step and means customers may not get the most recent data immediately. Option D publishes to Open Data, which makes the data freely and publicly available, contradicting the requirement to verify customer identities before sharing.

311.text:
Option C is correct because the application tier is stateless, so containerizing it with Docker and running it on Amazon ECS with EC2 capacity behind an Application Load Balancer makes the tier highly available and lets it scale on CPU utilization, directly resolving the 100% CPU saturation. Running on ECS replaces the manual in-place patching process, since instances and tasks are refreshed from updated images rather than patched live, eliminating the downtime the company experienced. Because the workload is stateless, this rehosting onto containers requires comparatively little development effort while delivering the required high availability.

Option A moves the application to AWS Lambda and migrates the database to Amazon DocumentDB, which is a substantial rewrite onto a serverless model and a non-relational document store. Option B changes to a Graviton instance type in an Auto Scaling group but migrates the MySQL data to Amazon DynamoDB, a NoSQL redesign requiring significant rework of data access. Option D rebuilds on an Auto Scaling group with a new SSM-enabled AMI and Aurora MySQL, but re-baking AMIs and re-architecting around launch templates is more development effort than rehosting the stateless tier into containers.

312.text:
Option C is correct because it gives every Region the ability to scale and routes users to the lowest-latency Region with the least operational overhead. An Auto Scaling group behind a Network Load Balancer in each Region scales EC2 capacity automatically to absorb load, and an Amazon Route 53 latency-based routing record automatically directs each user to the Region that offers the lowest latency. Storing the leaderboards, inventory, and event status in an Amazon DynamoDB global table provides fully managed, multi-active replication across all three Regions, so any Region can serve and update the shared state without custom replication code.

Option A uses RDS for MySQL with read replicas, which only serve reads from other Regions and require manual failover and replication management, unlike a DynamoDB global table's active-active writes. Option B runs self-managed MySQL on EC2 with custom replication and uses geoproximity routing, both of which add operational burden and do not directly optimize for lowest latency. Option D deploys custom DNS servers on EC2 with hand-built latency logic, which is far more complex and error-prone than native Route 53 latency-based routing.

313.text:
Option B is correct because resolving names from a private hosted zone requires three things working together. First, the private hosted zone must be explicitly associated with the VPC so Route 53 Resolver answers queries for that zone's namespace. Second, enableDnsSupport activates the Amazon-provided DNS resolver (at the VPC base +2 address), and enableDnsHostnames ensures instances with public IP addresses receive corresponding public DNS hostnames—satisfying the public hostname requirement. Third, the DHCP options set must direct instances to AmazonProvidedDNS so queries flow through the Route 53 Resolver that consults the associated private zone. This combination meets every stated requirement with native VPC DNS.

Option A is incorrect because it never associates the private hosted zone with the VPC, so the zone's records will not resolve, and hardcoding 10.24.34.2 is fragile compared to AmazonProvidedDNS. Option C is incorrect because deactivating enableDnsSupport disables the Amazon DNS resolver entirely, breaking both private hosted zone resolution and the resolver address it points to. Option D is incorrect because deactivating enableDnsHostnames prevents instances with public IP addresses from receiving public DNS hostnames, directly violating the requirement.

314.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Client VPN is a managed, client-based VPN that lets remote users—whether at home or in offices—establish secure connections into the VPC from their local machines. By associating the Client VPN endpoint with a subnet in the VPC, authenticated developers receive routable access to the OpenSearch Service domain, which keeps all data within the VPC as required. The self-service portal lets developers download the client and configuration without administrator involvement, and as a fully managed service it scales to a growing remote workforce with minimal operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN connects networks (sites), not individual remote laptops working from home; it does not provide per-user client connectivity, and a transit gateway adds unneeded complexity. Option C is incorrect because Direct Connect with a public VIF provides dedicated connectivity to AWS public endpoints, not private VPC access for individual developer machines, and it is expensive and slow to provision. Option D is incorrect because a bastion host only provides SSH access to instances; it does not give developers direct browser-based access to OpenSearch Dashboards from their local machines and adds maintenance burden.

315.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Database Migration Service performs an online migration using full load plus change data capture (CDC), which copies the existing 5 TB while continuously replicating ongoing changes—minimizing downtime since the source stays writable until cutover. Placing the replication instance in a private subnet and creating VPC endpoints for AWS DMS keeps traffic off the internet, and the data flows over the private VIF of the existing Direct Connect connection. KMS provides encryption at rest and TLS provides encryption in transit, satisfying both encryption requirements for the highly sensitive data.

Option A is incorrect because Snowball Edge is a physical shipping process that produces a static point-in-time backup; constantly updated data would be stale on import, causing significant downtime, and it is unnecessary given the existing Direct Connect link. Option C is incorrect because a backup plus DataSync is also a one-time bulk transfer with no change data capture, so updates after the backup are lost and downtime is large. Option D is incorrect for the same reason—an S3 File Gateway backup is a static snapshot; PrivateLink and TLS address privacy but the backup-and-import flow still incurs substantial downtime and does not capture continuous changes.

316.text:
Option B is correct because AWS WAF provides a managed geographic match rule that blocks or allows requests based on the country of origin derived from the source IP, requiring no manual IP list to maintain. Associating the web ACL with the Application Load Balancer enforces the rule at the edge of the application, and AWS WAF natively logs requests—including blocked ones—so the company can review denied access attempts. Because the geo-match condition is maintained by AWS, this is the lowest-maintenance solution that meets the country restriction and logging requirements.

Option A is incorrect because manually building and maintaining an IPSet of every IP range for a country is operationally heavy and error-prone, since IP allocations change constantly. Option C is incorrect because AWS Shield protects against DDoS attacks; it does not perform geographic request filtering or generate per-request block logs. Option D is incorrect because security groups filter by IP/port and cannot match by country, they do not provide request-level logging, and maintaining country IP ranges in a security group is impractical.

317.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Application Discovery Service with the agent-based data collection installs an agent on each virtual machine, and only the agent collects the full set of data the company needs: time-series metrics such as CPU, memory, and disk utilization; a detailed inventory of running processes; and inbound and outbound network connections that allow mapping of communications between servers. Because it captures process-level and network dependency data directly from inside each instance, it provides the deep visibility needed to size EC2 instances accurately while remaining cost-effective for this assessment.

Option C is incorrect because agentless discovery (via the OVA appliance in VMware environments) collects only VM-level performance metrics; it cannot enumerate running processes or capture network connection details, so it fails the inventory and dependency-mapping requirements. Option B is incorrect because deploying and managing the CloudWatch agent on every on-premises server adds operational overhead, ingest cost, and does not natively provide process inventory or server-to-server communication mapping. Option D is incorrect because Application Discovery Service does not operate as a console-driven network scanner over a VPN; that is not a supported collection mode.

318.text:
Options A, B, and D are correct because together they restore end-to-end visibility that survives instance termination during peak traffic. Configuring the Aurora MySQL cluster to publish slow query and error logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs (A) gives durable, centralized query diagnostics that the insufficient default metrics lacked. Implementing the AWS X-Ray SDK to trace incoming HTTP requests and instrument SQL queries with the X-Ray SDK for Java (B) provides distributed traces that pinpoint latency across the request path and into the database. Installing the CloudWatch Logs agent on the EC2 instances to ship Apache logs (D) ensures web server logs are persisted off the instances before Auto Scaling terminates them, solving the lost-log problem.

Option C is incorrect because streaming logs to Amazon Kinesis adds undifferentiated pipeline complexity when CloudWatch Logs already provides durable, analyzable storage. Option E is incorrect because CloudTrail records management API activity, not application request performance or query latency, so it does not help diagnose cart errors. Option F is incorrect because there is no Aurora feature that benchmarks the cluster and publishes a stream to X-Ray; X-Ray ingests trace segments from instrumented code, not a database benchmark feed.

319.text:
Option B is correct because the data loss occurs when the third-party pre-processing fails, so the solution must durably buffer incoming data before processing and retain anything that cannot be processed. Inserting a primary SQS queue as the API Gateway integration immediately persists every weather-station request, decoupling ingestion from the failing third-party call. The Lambda function consumes from the primary queue, and if processing repeatedly fails, the messages move to the secondary queue configured as the dead-letter queue. This guarantees no data is lost and that failed messages can be reprocessed later once the third-party service recovers.

Option A is incorrect because API Gateway does not support attaching an SQS dead-letter queue directly to the API; a DLQ is a property of a queue or asynchronous invocation, so this does not durably capture failures. Option C is incorrect because routing through EventBridge to a synchronously-behaving Lambda and a failure event bus is more complex and EventBridge buses are not designed as durable replayable buffers in this pattern. Option D is incorrect because configuring only a Lambda failure destination event bus does not buffer the original ingestion and does not provide a straightforward mechanism to reprocess the lost data later.

320.text:
Option D is correct because the root cause of slow failover is Route 53 DNS-based failover, which depends on health check evaluation plus DNS TTL expiry and client resolver caching—inherently slower than one minute. A CloudFront origin group with two origins, one per backend Region, and origin failover configured in the cache behavior moves failover into CloudFront itself: when CloudFront receives a failure status code or timeout from the primary origin, it immediately retries the secondary origin on the same request. This eliminates DNS propagation delay entirely and provides the fastest possible failover.

Option A is incorrect because adding a second CloudFront distribution and another Route 53 failover record still relies on DNS failover and TTL, so it does not reduce failover time. Option B is incorrect because lowering the TTL only marginally helps and still depends on health check intervals and resolver caching; sub-second DNS failover is not achievable and the question seeks the fastest design, not a tweak. Option C is incorrect because a latency routing policy optimizes for proximity, not failover, and does not provide deterministic origin failover behavior within CloudFront.

321.text:
Options B and D are correct because they offload both the file storage and the remote-access traffic from the saturated on-premises data center. Migrating the Windows home directories to Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed, native SMB file system with Active Directory integration, so users get their Windows home directories in AWS without the company maintaining file servers. Migrating remote users to AWS Client VPN gives them managed, scalable VPN connectivity directly into AWS rather than tunneling into the data center, which relieves the data center's connection bandwidth that is currently hitting 100%. Both are managed services, meeting the least-operational-overhead goal.

Option A is incorrect because a Storage Gateway Volume Gateway presents block (iSCSI) volumes and keeps the file server on premises, so remote-access traffic still flows into the saturated data center. Option C is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Lustre is a high-performance computing file system using POSIX/Lustre, not SMB, and cannot serve Windows home directories. Option E is incorrect because adding a Direct Connect connection increases capacity but adds cost and provisioning overhead and still routes user access through the data center rather than reducing dependence on it.
